hookRules.ruleYaml:
id: no-typealias-string
language: kotlin
rule:
  any:
    - pattern: public typealias $NAME = String
    - pattern: typealias $NAME = String
hookRules.rationale: Marcos's tenet bans `typealias X = String`; the SDK already ships validated value classes (ModelId/ProviderId). New `typealias … = String` re-introduces the TS string-union residue (PO-2, PO-4) with zero type separation. Validated against the codebase: matches GatewayModelId, VoyageEmbeddingModelId, etc. and nothing else.

hookRules.ruleYaml:
id: no-nullable-prompt-messages-bag
language: kotlin
rule:
  any:
    - pattern: require($A != null || $B.isNotEmpty())
    - pattern: require($A != null || $B.isNotEmpty()) { $$$MSG }
hookRules.rationale: Catches the TS option-bag cross-field invariant (NOB-1): a nullable scalar OR a possibly-empty collection guarded only by a runtime require(). Make illegal states unrepresentable with a sealed GenerationInput instead. Validated: matches exactly the three sites in Generate.kt and ToolLoopAgent.kt, no false positives.

hookRules.ruleYaml:
id: no-jsonnull-sentinel
language: kotlin
rule:
  pattern: $X ?: JsonNull
hookRules.rationale: Fabricated JsonNull sentinel for an absent value (SEAL-01, T5). `output ?: JsonNull` papers over a flat discriminated union where the type system should guarantee the payload. Validated: matches ConvertToModelMessages.kt 244/251/263 and a few provider sites — each is a real sentinel fabrication worth a block on new code.

hookRules.ruleYaml:
id: no-empty-string-sentinel
language: kotlin
rule:
  pattern: $X ?: ""
hookRules.rationale: Empty-string sentinel meaning 'absent' (SEAL-06, T5: no `.orEmpty()`/`?: ""` on required fields). null and "" collapse onto the same token. Warn (not block) because a handful of display-formatting uses can be legitimate; surfaces the pattern for review.

hookRules.ruleYaml:
id: no-public-mutable-var
language: kotlin
rule:
  pattern: public var $NAME: $TYPE
hookRules.rationale: Public mutable `var` is unsynchronized mutable shared state in a coroutine library (FW-2 AIProvider, FW-5 Completion/StructuredObject loading/error vars, GLOBAL state). Observable state should be StateFlow; identity should be val. Validated: matches the CompletionApi/Rsc/StructuredObject var sites that the audit flags. Warn so TelemetrySpan-style legitimate setters can be triaged.

T1: prefer `sealed class` with shared payload in the base constructor; reserve `sealed interface` only for multi-hierarchy membership (SEAL-03, SEAL-04). Warn-level nudge — the reviewer confirms no second-hierarchy need before converting. Validated: matches the domain unions (ContentPart/StreamEvent/UIMessagePart/Outcome) the audit cites.

FW-4: an error() method that re-throws its argument in the producer coroutine never reaches Flow collectors — a broken error channel masked as symmetry. Errors must propagate through the stream (emit a terminal event or fail the Flow). Validated: matches only Rsc.kt:43; zero false positives.

hookRules.ruleYaml:
id: no-provideroptions-jsonobject-cast
language: kotlin
rule:
  any:
    - pattern: providerOptions[$KEY] as? JsonObject
    - pattern: providerOptions[$KEY] as JsonObject
hookRules.rationale: raw-json-01: consuming the provider-config bag via `as? JsonObject` then string-literal key lookups is the Record<string,unknown> pattern with no compile check (typos no-op). Decode a typed @Serializable …ProviderOptions per provider slice instead. Scoped to the `providerOptions` receiver to stay low-false-positive.

hookRules.ruleYaml:
id: no-reflective-typeclass-cast
language: kotlin
rule:
  pattern: when ($T::class) { $$$ }
hookRules.rationale: GLOBAL-1: the `providerNotConfigured` facade uses a reflective `when (T::class) … as T` to fabricate throwing stubs — typed-over-reflection violation and a runtime landmine. Explicit construction/DI should replace reflective dispatch. Warn so genuine reified-class dispatch can be reviewed.

builderQueue.title: Make gateway metadata cache concurrency-safe (Mutex + single-flight)
builderQueue.instruction: In DefaultGatewayProvider replace the two plain vars `pendingMetadata`/`lastFetchTime` (Gateway.kt:261-262) with a single nullable `private data class MetadataCache(val response: GatewayFetchMetadataResponse, val fetchedAtMillis: Long)` field plus a `private val metadataMutex = Mutex()` (kotlinx.coroutines.sync). Rewrite `getAvailableModels()` (Gateway.kt:279-292) to run its read-check-fetch-store entirely inside `metadataMutex.withLock { }`, returning the cached response when `settings.metadataCacheRefreshMillis > 0 && now - fetchedAtMillis <= refresh`, else fetching via `settings.transport.getAvailableModels(requestContext())` and storing the new MetadataCache. This removes the data race AND de-dupes concurrent fetches (Mutex, not AtomicReference). Do NOT touch the public GatewayProvider interface or the throwing `gateway` singleton (that is FIX-010).
builderQueue.acceptance: Compiles + jvmTest green; add a test launching N concurrent getAvailableModels() coroutines asserting the transport is hit at most once within the refresh window; no ABI change (internal private impl) so api dumps unchanged.

builderQueue.title: Add GenerationInput-first overloads to generation entry points; deprecate the nullable pair
builderQueue.instruction: Promote KotlinApi's existing `sealed interface Input` (PromptText/MessageHistory/MessageHistoryWithPrompt + NonEmptyMessages + companion factories) to a top-level `public sealed class GenerationInput` at package level, keeping the three variants and NonEmptyMessages. Add new overloads of generateText (String and <TOutput> forms), streamText, streamTextResult, Agent.generate, Agent.stream taking `input: GenerationInput` as a NON-defaulted first parameter (before the optional params); their bodies build effectiveMessages via an exhaustive `when (input)` with NO require(). @Deprecated(level=WARNING, ReplaceWith) the existing nullable `prompt`/`messages` overloads, delegating to the new path via `GenerationInput.from(prompt, messages)`; keep the require()/IllegalArgumentException ONLY inside these shims and inside `from`. Add a compile test exercising both old (warns) and new shapes; verify no overload-resolution ambiguity for explicit `prompt =`/`messages =` callers.
builderQueue.acceptance: Compiles + jvmTest green; ABI dumps regenerated and reviewed to confirm they ONLY GAIN symbols (new overloads + GenerationInput) with all deprecated symbols retained → breaksAbi=false holds.

confirmed.title: Five web-framework packages (react/rsc/svelte/vue/angular) are TS package topology preserved as public KMP API — not idiomatic Kotlin
confirmed.tenet: T8 (feature-slice, no TS topology) / T6 (no speculative complexity) / T2 (don't leak foreign framework shapes into public API)

evidence.snippet: public typealias Chat = ai.torad.aisdk.ui.Chat  // entire 21-line package is typealias re-exports under a framework-named package
public typealias Chat = ai.torad.aisdk.ui.Chat   // svelte = 6 typealiases + AIContext(MutableMap holder)
import ai.torad.aisdk.react.UseChatOptions  // the ONLY consumer of these packages is a TS-parity test; no commonMain code uses them

confirmed.badPattern: Packages named after JS UI frameworks (react/rsc/svelte/vue/angular) holding React/Vue/Svelte/Angular hook vocabulary (useChat/useCompletion/experimental_useObject/useAIState/...) as first-class public API of a jvm/ios/linux/wasm Kotlin library. The hook naming has zero meaning without a React/Vue render cycle; in Kotlin these are stateless thin wrappers over ui.Chat / Completion / StructuredObject that add no capability. ast-grep + ABI dump confirm react/rsc are full classes in api/jvm/torad-aisdk.api (lines 12781-12921) and svelte/vue/angular are typealias-only re-exports. No commonMain consumer exists; only FrameworkFacadeParityTest references them, and it exists purely to assert TS package parity.
confirmed.solution: Delete the four pure-compat packages (svelte/vue/angular, plus the React-hook free functions in react) and collapse to ONE idiomatic Compose/Flow-friendly surface in ai.torad.aisdk.ui. Consumers should use ui.Chat / Completion / StructuredObject directly (or a single `rememberChat`-style Compose adapter living in an OPTIONAL androidMain/compose artifact, not commonMain). Concrete: keep ui.Chat as the canonical state-holder; expose its observable state as StateFlow (see FW-5); drop react/vue/svelte/angular entirely. If TS-porters need name familiarity, ship a separate `torad-aisdk-compat` artifact excluded from the stable ABI, not commonMain public surface. BEFORE: `ai.torad.aisdk.react.useChat(UseChatOptions(...)).messages`. AFTER: `ai.torad.aisdk.ui.Chat(id=..., transport=...).messagesFlow`.
confirmed.builderTaskHint: All symbols are @ExperimentalAiSdkApi (RequiresOptIn opt-in), so removal is permitted despite changing api/jvm/torad-aisdk.api and api/torad-aisdk.klib.api — regen both dumps after deletion. Delete svelte/Svelte.kt, vue/Vue.kt, angular/Angular.kt wholesale; gut react/React.kt to nothing (or keep only as deprecated shims for one release). Also delete src/commonTest/.../FrameworkFacadeParityTest.kt which only tests TS parity. Verify nothing in commonMain breaks (grep already confirms only codemod references these names as strings).
confirmed.dimension: TS framework packages in a KMP library

verdict.critique: VERIFIED REAL by opening every cited file. react/React.kt:75 useChat matches verbatim; angular/Angular.kt is 22 lines of pure typealias re-exports (typealias Chat at line 6); svelte/Svelte.kt:7 typealias Chat + AIContext holding two MutableMaps; vue/Vue.kt re-uses react.* helpers. The packages are JS-framework topology (react/rsc/svelte/vue/angular) carrying render-cycle hook vocabulary (useChat/useCompletion/experimental_useObject/useAIState/useUIState/getMutableAIState) as first-class public API of a jvm/ios/linux/wasm Kotlin library — meaningless without a React/Vue/Svelte render cycle, and adding zero capability over ui.Chat/Completion/StructuredObject. Confirmed via ast-grep-equivalent reads: react/rsc are full ABI classes (api/jvm/torad-aisdk.api ~12781+), svelte/vue/angular are typealias-only. The ONLY consumer is FrameworkFacadeParityTest (a TS-parity test); the lone commonMain reference is codemod/Codemod.kt:48-52 which uses the names only as STRING literals in a find/replace migration rule (rewriteFrameworkImports), not real code — so there is no functional consumer. This squarely violates T8 (TS topology, not feature-slice), T6 (speculative/JS-ecosystem surface no Kotlin caller needs), T2 (foreign framework shapes leaking into public API). TWO ACCURACY GAPS in the finding: (1) it calls rsc a typealias-only/full-class twin, but rsc is NOT typealias re-exports — it contains genuinely Flow-native primitives (StreamableValueController backed by MutableSharedFlow replay=64/DROP_OLDEST, createStreamableValue/readStreamableValue) alongside the React-state shims (AIProvider with mutable var aiState, useAIState/useUIState). The deletion plan names svelte/vue/angular+react explicitly but is vague on rsc, which is the package needing the most surgical (not wholesale) treatment. (2) Minor: snippet count details ('svelte = 6 typealiases') are approximately right. ABI assessment is correct: deleting these public symbols IS a binary break (breaksAbi=true), but every symbol is @ExperimentalAiSdkApi (RequiresOptIn ERROR, BINARY retention, OptIn.kt:24) whose own KDoc declares them removable 'without going through the normal deprecation cycle' — so removal is policy-permitted despite the ABI dump churn, exactly as the builderTaskHint states. Both api/jvm and api/klib dumps must be regenerated after deletion.
verdict.refinedSolution: Adopt the original direction (collapse to ONE idiomatic ai.torad.aisdk.ui surface) with these precise corrections: (1) DELETE wholesale: svelte/Svelte.kt, vue/Vue.kt, angular/Angular.kt — they are pure typealias/MutableMap re-exports with no logic. (2) react/React.kt: delete the React-hook free functions (useChat/useCompletion/experimental_useObject) and the *Helpers wrappers — they add nothing over ui.Chat / Completion / StructuredObject. Keep nothing, or at most one-release @Deprecated(level=WARNING, ReplaceWith) shims since the symbols are already opt-in. (3) rsc is NOT pure compat — handle it surgically, not by the same wholesale delete: the React-Server-Component STATE vocabulary (AIProvider with mutable var aiState/uiState, createAI/getAIState/getMutableAIState/useAIState/useUIState/useActions/useSyncUIState/useStreamableValue) is pure TS topology and mutable-holder anti-pattern — DELETE it. But the Flow-native streamable primitives (StreamableValueController/StreamableValue/createStreamableValue/readStreamableValue, StreamableUIController, createAgentUIStream) are genuinely idiomatic Kotlin (MutableSharedFlow, Flow returns) — if any real consumer needs them, RELOCATE+RENAME them into ai.torad.aisdk.ui (e.g. ui.StreamableValue / streamableValueFlow) stripped of the RSC name; otherwise delete. Do not leave them stranded under an rsc package. (4) Delete src/commonTest/.../FrameworkFacadeParityTest.kt (asserts only TS parity). (5) Delete the codemod 'rewriteFrameworkImports' CodemodRule (Codemod.kt:48-52) and remove it from CodemodRules.all — once the packages are gone, rewriting @ai-sdk/react -> ai.torad.aisdk.react would point migrators at deleted symbols; this is the orphan your own change creates and must be cleaned per Surgical-Changes. verdict.critique:
Evidence fully verified by reading the file and the ABI. Rsc.kt:43-45 is exactly `error(throwable) { throw throwable }`; the sibling at 86-94 emits a UIMessagePart.Error into the stream — genuinely contradictory error semantics for two controllers built from the same template. The .api file confirms StreamableValueController (incl. the error method) is frozen into the JVM ABI. The finding is real and correctly diagnosed.

The finding actually UNDERSTATES the problem: both controllers expose a MutableSharedFlow, which has no terminal state and no close(cause). So `done()` does not complete the stream (it only emits a final value), and `error()` fundamentally cannot propagate to collectors via a SharedFlow no matter what. `throw throwable` in a suspend fun just rethrows in the producer's own call frame — a no-op the caller could have written themselves. So error() is not merely 'inconsistent', it is dead.

abiAssessment: breaksAbi=true is correct — the symbol is in the .api file and any change/removal is a strict binary break. The builderTaskHint correctly notes @ExperimentalAiSdkApi (a RequiresOptIn marker, confirmed in OptIn.kt) means the library policy permits the break. Both statements are accurate.

Solution caveat (why not fully clean): the recommended 'back the value with a Channel and channel.close(throwable)' is imprecise. This controller is a hot, multi-subscriber, replay=64 fan-out. A plain Channel is single-consumer; channelFlow/callbackFlow are cold and re-run per collector — either swap would silently regress the replay/multi-subscriber behavior the current SharedFlow provides. The PREFERRED recommendation (delete the rsc/RSC-port surface) and the minimum ('make error() propagate through the stream') are both correct, so I accept the solution, but the Channel detail should not be taken literally for a multi-subscriber controller.

Generate.kt:20-23 and 114 (the require lives in the shared private generateTextImpl, reached by both public generateText overloads); Generate.kt:396 (streamTextCallParams); ToolLoopAgent.kt:476 (streamInternal flow body, also reached by generate via streamInternal); Agent.kt:75-81 and 84-90 (generate + stream). Genuine T5 (nullable-pair invariant) + T1 (no sealed discriminated input) violation, 11 occurrences plausible across the public surface.

The solution is a real fix, not a half-measure: it makes a sealed input type the PRIMARY parameter and demotes the nullable overloads to @Deprecated(WARNING) shims with the require() confined to those shims — exactly the additive + deprecate migration this published explicitApi library wants. breaksAbi=false is correct: deprecated functions remain in the ABI, api/*.api only gains symbols.

Two accuracy nits in the solution, neither fatal: (1) It proposes a NEW type GenerationInput with variant names FromPrompt/FromMessages/FromBoth, but KotlinApi.kt:160 ALREADY defines a sealed Input with PromptText/MessageHistory/MessageHistoryWithPrompt + NonEmptyMessages. Inventing a parallel third type creates two competing sealed hierarchies (a dependency-scan "two patterns coexisting" smell). Better to PROMOTE the existing one. (2) The existing Input is a sealed INTERFACE — the solution correctly calls for sealed class per T1, which is the right call, but it should explicitly note this is a conversion of the existing interface, and that the existing public Input.from / TextGenerationRequest nullable primary constructor (KotlinApi.kt:125-127, 198) must ALSO be deprecated, else the bad nullable pair survives on the request type even after the function signatures are fixed.
verdict.refinedSolution:
Promote, do not duplicate. KotlinApi.kt already contains the exhaustive design (sealed interface Input with PromptText / MessageHistory / MessageHistoryWithPrompt, plus the NonEmptyMessages value type and companion factories). Do this instead of inventing a parallel GenerationInput:

1. Convert KotlinApi.kt's `sealed interface Input` to a top-level `public sealed class GenerationInput` (T1: sealed class with the shared `prompt`/`messages` accessors). Keep the three variants and NonEmptyMessages; move them to package level so generateText/streamText/Agent can share one type. No multi-hierarchy need, so sealed class is correct.

confirmed.dimension: Sealed modeling: interface-vs-class, flat unions, Any? erasure

verdict.critique:
Evidence verified by reading all three files. ContentPart (ModelMessage.kt:68) is a sealed interface; providerMetadata is repeated on 8 of 9 variants (ToolApprovalResponse omits it). StreamEvent (Streaming.kt:48) is a sealed interface with 24 variants, ~18 carrying providerMetadata. UIMessagePart (ui/UIMessagePart.kt:51) is a sealed interface; 8 of 10 variants carry it (minor miscount: finding says "8 of 9", actually 10 variants). The interface-vs-class observation and the T1 tenet citation are accurate, and the finding correctly establishes that no variant needs multi-hierarchy membership (the SourcePart.SourceType reference is type reuse, not union membership), so the sealed-class preference genuinely applies. isReal=true; tenetAligned=true.

The SOLUTION is wrong, however. It prescribes hoisting providerMetadata to an abstract base property across all three unions ("Same treatment for StreamEvent and UIMessagePart"). But providerMetadata is NOT universally shared. The Streaming.kt header (lines 24-25) DELIBERATELY excludes it from the control singletons (StreamStart, Abort — a data object), the terminal Error, Raw, Finish, and ResponseMetadata; ContentPart excludes it from ToolApprovalResponse; UIMessagePart from Error and StepStart. A blanket abstract-base hoist forces a meaningless always-null providerMetadata onto exactly those variants — fabricating a field whose absence is the design, which is itself a T5 violation (an Abort/Error that can carry provider metadata is an illegal state made representable). The solution glosses over this and contradicts the codebase's own documented intent for 2 of 3 cited unions. solutionCorrect=false.

The ABI assessment is correct: sealed interface to sealed class changes the supertype kind in the klib ABI for every variant — source-compatible for when/construction, binary-incompatible — and adding an abstract property also breaks ABI. breaksAbi=true is right. Worth flagging: this is a pure tenet/style change that CANNOT be done additively or via @Deprecated, so for a published Maven Central library it forces an ABI rebaseline for marginal benefit; the cost/benefit is weak and the builderTaskHint's "lower severity" framing is fair.
verdict.refinedSolution:
Split the fix into two independent decisions instead of one blanket hoist.

1) interface to class (T1 alignment): Convert each `sealed interface` to `sealed class` (e.g. `public sealed class ContentPart { ... data class Text(...) : ContentPart() }`). This is the genuine T1 win and needs no multi-hierarchy. Keep all @SerialName/@JsonClassDiscriminator tags unchanged so the wire format stays byte-stable. ABI-breaking (supertype kind change) — must land as a single ABI rebaseline, sequenced with SEAL-01/02 which also touch UIMessagePart.

2) providerMetadata hoist — do NOT do it blindly. The field is intentionally absent on control/terminal variants (Abort/Error/Raw/StreamStart/ResponseMetadata/Finish; ToolApprovalResponse; UI Error/StepStart), documented in Streaming.kt lines 24-25. Forcing an abstract base property onto them fabricates a meaningless null field (T5 violation). Two acceptable options:
   a) Leave providerMetadata per-variant. Its non-universality is the point — keeping it off the control/terminal variants makes illegal states unrepresentable. The "DRY" duplication is shallow (one identical line) and the current shape is more correct than a forced hoist. Given the ABI cost, this is the recommended minimal change: do (1) only.
   b) If polymorphic access is genuinely needed, introduce an intermediate grouping that ONLY the metadata-carrying variants extend (e.g. `sealed class ContentPart` with a nested `sealed class WithMetadata : ContentPart() { abstract val providerMetadata: Map<String, JsonElement>? }`, and have Text/Reasoning/... extend `WithMetadata`). This gives `(part as? WithMetadata)?.providerMetadata` without forcing the field onto Abort/Error/Raw. More structure, but it preserves the deliberate exclusion.

Recommendation: ship (1) alone if the ABI break is accepted; skip the providerMetadata hoist entirely. Reject the finding's blanket-hoist as written — it regresses the deliberate "no metadata on control/terminal events" invariant. Given this is an additive-impossible ABI break on a published library for a style-only gain, also surface to Marcos whether the binary-compat cost is worth it before doing even (1).

evidence.snippet: public data class DynamicToolUI(val toolCallId: String, val toolName: String, val state: ToolCallState, val input: JsonElement?=null, val output: JsonElement?=null, val error: String?=null, val preliminary: Boolean=false, ...) : UIMessagePart  // ToolUI minus approvalId/signature

public fun <TOutput> dynamicOutputAs(part: UIMessagePart.DynamicToolUI, serializer: KSerializer<TOutput>): TOutput? { if (part.state != ToolCallState.OutputAvailable) return null; val raw = part.output ?: return null ... }  // verbatim copy of outputAs for ToolUI

confirmed.badPattern: ToolUI (static) and DynamicToolUI (runtime-typed) differ only by a `dynamic` axis and the presence of approval fields, yet are two full copies of the same nullable flat union, each requiring its own outputAs/inputAs (UIMessagePart.kt:218-268, four near-identical functions). The duplication multiplies the SEAL-01 flat-union bug across two types and guarantees they drift.
confirmed.solution: After SEAL-01 sealifies the state, collapse the two into ONE type discriminated by a `dynamic: Boolean` (or a `kind: Static/Dynamic`) on a shared sealed state, since the lifecycle State is identical. BEFORE: separate `ToolUI` + `DynamicToolUI` data classes + `outputAs`/`dynamicOutputAs`/`inputAs`/`dynamicInputAs`. AFTER: single `data class ToolUI(val toolCallId: String, val toolName: String, val dynamic: Boolean, val state: State)` with one generic `fun ToolUI.outputAs(serializer)` reading `state as? State.OutputAvailable`. Renderers branch on `part.dynamic` for the fallback card instead of `is DynamicToolUI`.
confirmed.builderTaskHint: ABI break (removes a public type + 4 public functions). Do this in the SAME pass as SEAL-01 so UIMessagePart's ABI is rebaselined once. If full unification is too aggressive for one PR, at minimum make DynamicToolUI share SEAL-01's sealed State so the flat-union fix isn't written twice.
confirmed.dimension: Sealed modeling: interface-vs-class, flat unions, Any? erasure

verdict.critique:
EVIDENCE CONFIRMED (isReal=true). I opened UIMessagePart.kt and verified every cited line. ToolUI (lines 72-103) and DynamicToolUI (lines 199-208) are two separate `data class` declarations sharing the identical nullable flat-union payload (toolCallId, toolName, state: ToolCallState, input/output: JsonElement?, error: String?, preliminary, providerMetadata); DynamicToolUI differs only by omitting approvalId/signature. The four near-identical helpers are real: outputAs (218), inputAs (231), dynamicOutputAs (246), dynamicInputAs (258) — dynamic* are verbatim copies of the static bodies. The klib.api confirms all of these are exported public surface. So the structural fork is genuine and the duplication will drift.

ABI ASSESSMENT CORRECT (true). Collapsing the two types removes a public type (UIMessagePart.DynamicToolUI), its generated $serializer/Companion, copy/componentN, and the dynamicOutputAs/dynamicInputAs top-level functions — all present in api/torad-aisdk.klib.api (lines 1334+, 14033) and the jvm .api. That is unambiguously a binary break for this explicitApi() Maven Central library. breaksAbi=true is right, and the builderTaskHint to rebaseline in the same pass as SEAL-01 is sound sequencing.

SOLUTION WRONG (solutionCorrect=false, tenetAligned=false). Two problems:

1) The PRIMARY recommended shape — a single `data class ToolUI(..., val dynamic: Boolean, val state: State)` — replaces one bad axis (a type fork) with another anti-tenet axis: a Boolean discriminator. T1 is explicit: sealed CLASS over boolean/interface flags, make the discriminant a type, exhaustive `when`. A `dynamic: Boolean` is precisely the flat-flag pattern the tenets reject; renderers branching on `part.dynamic` instead of `is X` is a step sideways, not up. The finding hedges with "or a kind: Static/Dynamic", but that's still a flag field rather than a real subtype hierarchy.

2) More seriously, full collapse ERODES a real invariant. The current separation makes approvalId/signature UNREPRESENTABLE for dynamic tools (T5: illegal states unrepresentable). The proposed AFTER drops approvalId/signature off the merged type entirely (relocated into a sealed State per SEAL-01), which means a dynamic tool could now reach an approval-bearing state that the static/dynamic split previously forbade — unless that constraint is re-encoded, which the solution does not address. Merging on a boolean cannot express "approval applies to static only."

The correct fix that honors T1/T5 AND removes the duplication is a sealed CLASS, not a boolean: a `sealed class ToolUI` with the shared payload in the base constructor and `ToolUI.Static` (adds approvalId/signature) / `ToolUI.Dynamic` subclasses. ONE generic `inputAs`/`outputAs` operates on the base, killing the dynamic* copies, while the subtype split keeps the approval invariant and gives an exhaustive `when` at the render seam. This is strictly better than both the status quo and the finding's proposal.

NOTE: the helper-duplication half of this finding (dynamicOutputAs/dynamicInputAs being verbatim copies) is valid and fixable INDEPENDENTLY of SEAL-01 — those two functions read only the shared base fields (state, input, output) and could collapse to one generic pair the moment a shared base/supertype exposes those fields, regardless of whether ToolCallState gets sealified.

confirmed.title: ContentPart.File/Image/Source encode their data-shape union via empty-string sentinel (base64="") + nullable url, and an enum-gated nullable bag — invalid combinations representable
confirmed.tenet: T5 (no sentinel fabrication, no enum+nullable flat union), T1 (sealed shape)

evidence.snippet: public data class File(val mediaType: String, val base64: String = "", val filename: String? = null, ..., val url: String? = null) : ContentPart  // base64="" sentinel means 'absent', url=null means 'absent' — both-empty and both-set are legal
public data class Image(val mediaType: String, val base64: String = "", ..., val url: String? = null) : ContentPart

public data class Source(val sourceType: SourceType, val url: String? = null, val title: String? = null, val mediaType: String? = null, val filename: String? = null) : ContentPart  // Url vs Document gated by enum, payload fields all nullable

confirmed.badPattern: A content payload that is genuinely 'inline bytes OR remote URL' is modeled as `base64: String = ""` (empty-string sentinel for absent) alongside `url: String? = null` (null for absent) — so `File(base64="", url=null)` (nothing) and `File(base64=set, url=set)` (ambiguous) both compile. Likewise ContentPart.Source uses a `sourceType` enum to gate which of url / mediaType / filename are meaningful, with all of them nullable. Doc comments admit it: 'Currently base64 is the only data shape; a URL-shaped variant lives in Source' (line 177).
confirmed.solution: Model the data shape as a sealed class so exactly one form is present. BEFORE: `data class File(val mediaType: String, val base64: String="", val url: String?=null)`. AFTER: `data class File(val mediaType: String, val data: Data, val filename: String?=null) { sealed class Data { data class Inline(val base64: String) : Data(); data class Remote(val url: String) : Data() } }`; same for Image. For Source, split by the enum it already carries: `sealed class Source : ContentPart { data class Url(val url: String, val title: String?) : Source(); data class Document(val mediaType: String, val filename: String?, val title: String?) : Source() }` (UIMessagePart already did exactly this split — SourceUrl vs SourceDocument at UIMessagePart.kt:121,136 — so ContentPart is the inconsistent laggard).
confirmed.builderTaskHint: ABI + wire break (base64/url getters disappear; @Serializable shape changes). UIMessagePart.SourceUrl/SourceDocument (UIMessagePart.kt:121,136) is the in-repo proof the team already chose the sealed split for the UI side — this finding is just propagating it to the domain ContentPart side. NOTE ON TRUNCATION: I capped at 6 findings. Two lower-severity items were intentionally dropped: (a) StreamEvent.SourcePart (Streaming.kt:118) is the same enum+nullable flat union as ContentPart.Source and should ride along with SEAL-06; (b) StreamEvent.ToolResult (Streaming.kt:186) carries output + modelOutput + isError as three overlapping fields where isError is derivable from modelOutput — a minor pre-computed-redundancy smell, not an illegal-state bug.
confirmed.dimension: Sealed modeling: interface-vs-class, flat unions, Any? erasure

verdict.critique: VERIFIED by reading the files. ModelMessage.kt:184-196 File has base64: String = "" (empty-string sentinel for absent) alongside url: String? = null; :212-222 Image is identical; :163-172 Source gates url/title/mediaType/filename (all nullable) behind a sourceType enum. The doc comment at :177 explicitly admits the design gap. File(base64="", url=null) and File(base64=set, url=set) both compile — illegal/ambiguous states representable. T5 violation (sentinel + nullable-pair) is real. In-repo precedent confirmed: UIMessagePart.kt:121/:136 SourceUrl/SourceDocument is already the sealed split with distinct non-nullable fields and no enum gate, so ContentPart.Source is the documented inconsistent laggard. Two caveats, neither fatal: (1) the finding labels this T1 "sealed shape" but ContentPart is a sealed INTERFACE (line 68) and its variants are already sealed members — the actual defect is intra-variant field modeling, not interface-vs-class; the T1 framing is slightly imprecise though T5 carries it. (2) The proposed solution is directionally correct and NOT a half-measure (it replaces base64/url with data: Data rather than adding alongside), but it omits the kotlinx.serialization wiring a nested @Serializable sealed payload requires, and it ignores the CONTEXT mandate to prefer additive + @Deprecated migration for a published ABI-tracked library. breaksAbi=true is correct: getters disappear and the @Serializable/wire shape changes, both tracked in api/*.api.

confirmed.title: Tool-correlation ids (toolCallId / toolName / approvalId) are raw, adjacent, swappable Strings across the whole tool/message/stream surface
confirmed.tenet: T5 (exhaustive, no primitive ids) + value-classes-over-typealias-String; this is the real "ids that get passed around and confused" case the prior report (finding #18) MISSED — it only flagged the cosmetic per-provider model-id aliases.

evidence.snippet: public fun toolMessage(toolCallId: String, toolName: String, output: JsonElement): ModelMessage = ModelMessage(
listOf(ContentPart.ToolResult(toolCallId, toolName, output)),  // two adjacent String args, positionally swappable, no compiler guard

public data class ToolResult(
        val toolCallId: String,
        val toolName: String,
        val output: JsonElement,
val approvalId: String? = null,  // "distinct from toolCallId" yet same raw type, defaults to it — nothing stops mixing the two
public val toolCallId: String,  // ToolExecutionOptions — same raw String flows into every tool body

confirmed.badPattern: Domain correlation ids modeled as bare `String`: 54 `toolCallId: String`, 44 `toolName: String`, 20 `approvalId: String` occurrences across ContentPart.{ToolCall,ToolResult,ToolApprovalRequest,ToolApprovalResponse}, StreamEvent.*, UIMessagePart.{ToolUI,DynamicToolUI}, ToolLoopAgentAction.* and the factory functions. Because `ToolCall`/`ToolResult` declare `toolCallId` and `toolName` as adjacent String fields and the factories pass them positionally (ModelMessage.kt:33), swapping id and name compiles cleanly and corrupts tool-result correlation at runtime.
confirmed.builderTaskHint: ABI-breaking and wide (118 sites across ModelMessage, Streaming/StreamEvent, ui/UIMessage, Tool, ToolLoopAgent). On JVM, value-class params/getters get name-mangled (e.g. `getModelId-o4H4ZZ8`) so api/jvm/*.api and api/*.klib.api will both diff — regenerate dumps. Do ToolCallId + ToolName + ApprovalId in ONE pass (they co-occur) to avoid half-migration. Verify wire compat with a serialization round-trip test asserting `Json.encodeToString(ToolResult(...))` still emits `"toolCallId":"..."` as a bare string. This is the single highest-value finding in this dimension.
confirmed.dimension: String typealiases & primitive obsession

verdict.critique:
Evidence is real and verified by reading the files. ModelMessage.kt:87-88 (ToolCall) and 114-115 (ToolResult) declare toolCallId/toolName as adjacent same-type String fields; toolMessage() passes them positionally at line 33 — swapping compiles and silently corrupts tool-result correlation. Counts are exact: 54 toolCallId + 44 toolName + 20 approvalId = 118. Tool.kt:1097 confirmed (the finding labels its enclosing class "ToolExecutionOptions" but it is actually ToolExecutionContext — a cosmetic mislabel, the cited field exists). ModelRef.kt confirms the @JvmInline value-class convention the solution cites.

The solution is correct and not a half-measure: it replaces String with value classes at every site rather than adding a parallel good type. Two accuracy refinements: (1) ModelRef's ProviderId/ModelId are @JvmInline but NOT @Serializable — they never cross the wire. The finding's solution correctly diverges by adding @Serializable, which is exactly what makes the v6 JSON stay byte-identical (a @Serializable @JvmInline value class wrapping a single String serializes transparently to the bare string). This is the load-bearing detail and it is right. (2) breaksAbi=true is correct — both api/jvm/torad-aisdk.api and api/torad-aisdk.klib.api will diff (JVM getter/param name-mangling plus klib signature change), and the builder hint to do all three ids in one pass and add a round-trip serialization test is sound.

Tenet alignment: aligns with T5 (typed ids over primitive obsession) and value-classes-over-typealias-String; preserves T2 (wire shape unchanged via transparent serialization). No sealed-class/Flow/global-state concerns apply. One adjacent T5 smell the finding does not address (out of scope but worth noting): approvalId: String? = null with doc semantics "null defaults to toolCallId" is a null-means-two-things pattern; making it ApprovalId? preserves that nullability rather than resolving it.

This is a legitimate high-value finding that the prior report's model-id-alias finding (#18) did miss.

confirmed.title: 85 `typealias <Provider><Kind>ModelId = String` coexist with the SDK's own `ModelId`/`ProviderId` value classes — a half-finished migration
confirmed.tenet: value-classes/sealed over `typealias X = String`; T6 (the right structural type already exists, the aliases are speculative TS-union residue). Prior report finding #18 is CONFIRMED (85 aliases, exact cited lines match) but undersells it: it doesn't note the value class already exists and is bypassed.

evidence.snippet:
@JvmInline
public value class ModelId(public val value: String) { init { require(value.isNotBlank()) ... } }  // the canonical, validated type EXISTS
public typealias GatewayModelId = String
public typealias AnthropicMessagesModelId = String
public operator fun invoke(modelId: AnthropicMessagesModelId): LanguageModel = languageModel(modelId)  // takes String alias, NOT the ModelId value class
val buildRequestUrl: ((baseURL: String, modelId: String, isStreaming: Boolean) -> String)? = null,  // same file switches between the alias and raw String — proof the alias enforces nothing

confirmed.badPattern: Two parallel representations of "a model id": (a) `ModelId`/`ProviderId` validated `@JvmInline value class` in ModelRef.kt with extension overloads `Provider.languageModel(modelId: ModelId)`, and (b) 85 `public typealias <X>ModelId = String` that every concrete provider's `invoke`/`chat`/`languageModel` actually uses. The aliases add zero type separation (any String, including a toolCallId, satisfies them), provide no validation, and are pure TS string-literal-union residue. They don't even alias to `ModelId`.
confirmed.builderTaskHint: The additive route (deprecate aliases + add ModelId overloads) is NOT ABI-breaking: typealiases never appear in the klib/jvm ABI dumps (they expand to kotlin/String — `grep -c typealias api/*.klib.api` = 0), and new overloads are additive. A full migration that REPLACES the String param of every `invoke`/`chat` with `ModelId` WOULD break ABI (param type + JVM mangling). Recommend additive. Lower-value sub-aliases I am NOT separately itemizing but which fall under the same fix: ElevenLabsSpeechVoiceId, GoogleInteractionsAgentName, BlackForestLabsAspectRatio (open-valued, not a closed set), and the remaining ~80 per-provider model-id aliases.
confirmed.dimension: String typealiases & primitive obsession

verdict.critique:
Every cited line was opened and verified verbatim. ModelRef.kt:16 — `@JvmInline public value class ModelId(public val value: String)` with `require(value.isNotBlank())` — exists exactly as quoted; ModelRef.kt:62 `Provider.languageModel(modelId: ModelId)` extension exists. Gateway.kt:9 `public typealias GatewayModelId = String` confirmed. AnthropicProvider.kt:32 `public typealias AnthropicMessagesModelId = String` confirmed; :45 `buildRequestUrl: ((baseURL: String, modelId: String, ...))` uses raw String in the same file while :56 `invoke(modelId: AnthropicMessagesModelId)` uses the alias — the inconsistency the finding cites is real. Counts: `grep -rhoE "public typealias [A-Za-z]+ = String"` over commonMain = 85 (81 of them `...ModelId`), matching the claimed 85 with the remainder being VoiceId/AgentName/AspectRatio etc. ABI verification: `grep -c typealias` over both api/*.klib.api and api/jvm/torad-aisdk.api = 0, so the aliases expand to kotlin/String and never surface in the ABI surface — the additive deprecation route genuinely does NOT break binary compat, and ModelId is exported as a value class in the JVM ABI (box/unbox + mangled getModelId-o4H4ZZ8). breaksAbi=false is correct.

The pattern is genuine and material: a typealias to String in Kotlin provides zero autocomplete (unlike the TS string-literal unions they were mechanically ported from) and zero type separation — a toolCallId String satisfies AnthropicMessagesModelId. The validated ModelId value class already exists and is bypassed by every concrete provider entrypoint. This is exactly T6 residue plus the tenet's value-class-over-`typealias X = String` rule.

On solution quality: the proposed additive path (`@Deprecated(ReplaceWith("ModelId"))` each alias + add `invoke(modelId: ModelId)` overloads beside the kept `invoke(String)`) is NOT a half-measure — deprecation establishes a removal trajectory rather than blessing the bad type permanently, which is the correct posture for a published explicitApi library (tenet: additive + @Deprecated over silent breakage). It compiles (String and ModelId are distinct overloads; the typealias is just String), touches no Flow/suspend contract, and the sealed known/custom alternative is correctly scoped to genuinely closed id sets while acknowledging most provider id sets are open-valued. Minor gaps: (1) the finding doesn't flag that bare string-literal call sites will continue to resolve to invoke(String) silently, so the @Deprecated on the alias alone won't nudge callers who pass raw strings — the deprecation that actually drives migration must sit on the String-typed overloads, not (only) on the alias; (2) ModelId validates only non-blank, so migrating per-provider aliases to one ModelId loses nothing (the per-provider distinction was never enforced) but also adds little beyond non-blank validation — fine, but worth stating so reviewers don't expect provider-scoping.

confirmed.title: Core provider/model abstraction uses raw `String` identity instead of the existing `ModelId`/`ProviderId` value classes
confirmed.tenet: value-classes over primitive ids; T3 (the abstraction is where typing pays off — callers route on identity). The base interfaces erase the very types the SDK defined, so the 85 aliases and the value classes both collapse to String at the seam.

evidence.snippet: public val modelId: String  // not ModelId
public val provider: String  // not ProviderId; default get() = "unknown"
public val providerId: String  // not ProviderId
public fun languageModel(modelId: String): LanguageModel = throw NoSuchModelError(providerId, "language", modelId)

confirmed.badPattern: `LanguageModel.modelId: String`, `LanguageModel.provider: String`, `Provider.providerId: String`, and the eight `Provider.<kind>Model(modelId: String)` factories all use raw String for identity that the SDK has already given dedicated value classes (`ModelId`, `ProviderId`). The typed `ModelRef` overloads in ModelRef.kt just unwrap to `.value` and call back into the String API, so the strong types never reach the abstraction boundary.
confirmed.builderTaskHint: ABI-breaking: getter return types change from `Ljava/lang/String;` to the boxed value class and value-class params get JVM name-mangling (see existing `languageModel-6qrPkTc` in api/jvm/torad-aisdk.api:3967). Every provider impl (35 files in providers/) overrides these members, so this is a coordinated change. Given the 1.0 ABI freeze, consider deferring to a major-version bump OR doing the additive-overload-only variant (add ModelId members with defaults, keep String members) to avoid the break — flag the keep-vs-break decision to the user.
confirmed.dimension: String typealiases & primitive obsession

verdict.critique: Evidence verified by reading the files, not just grep. LanguageModel.kt:25 (`modelId: String`), :36-37 (`provider: String` default `get() = "unknown"`), Provider.kt:4 (`providerId: String`), Provider.kt:6 (`languageModel(modelId: String)` throwing NoSuchModelError) all say exactly what the finding claims. The value classes ProviderId/ModelId do exist (ModelRef.kt:6,16), and the typed overloads (ModelRef.kt:62-81) genuinely just call `.value` and re-enter the String API, so the strong types never cross the abstraction seam — the finding's central thesis. ABI mangling is real: `languageModel-6qrPkTc` is present at api/jvm/torad-aisdk.api:3967, so changing interface member types/params would re-mangle JVM names and break ABI. The proposed fix makes ModelId/ProviderId load-bearing on the interface and demotes the String form to a @Deprecated shim — a real fix, not a half-measure that keeps the bad primary. Two caveats. (1) Minor miscount: the finding says 'eight' Provider factories; there are seven model kinds. (2) The builderTaskHint references a '1.0 ABI freeze' and floats an additive-only variant (add ModelId members, keep String) to avoid the break — but the codebase is at 0.3.0-alpha01, i.e. PRE-1.0, so breaking cleanly now is both permitted and preferable to baking the String API into the 1.0 frozen surface. Deferring or going additive-only here would entrench the primitive-obsessed seam the finding correctly identifies.

confirmed.title: `QuiverAIOperation = String` is a closed two-value set that should be an enum/sealed
confirmed.tenet: T1 (closed sets are sealed/enum, exhaustive `when`), T5 (no sentinel `?: "generate"` fallback fabrication).

evidence.snippet: public typealias QuiverAIOperation = String
val operation = options["operation"]?.jsonPrimitive?.contentOrNull ?: "generate"  // sentinel fallback on a stringly-typed closed set
if (operation == "generate") "/svgs/generations" else "/svgs/vectorizations"  // binary set: generate | vectorization

confirmed.badPattern: `QuiverAIOperation` is aliased to `String` but the code only ever branches on a closed binary set (`"generate"` vs vectorization), read out of a `Map<String,JsonElement>` with a `?: "generate"` sentinel default. A typo in `"generate"` silently routes to vectorization.
confirmed.builderTaskHint: Removing the `public typealias` itself is NOT an ABI break (it expands to String and isn't in the dumps), but introducing the enum changes the public type that any signature using `QuiverAIOperation` exposes. Low severity / isolated to one provider. This is the last finding: I am intentionally NOT separately itemizing the other ~80 model-id aliases (covered by PO-2 as a class) nor the open-valued aliases (BlackForestLabsAspectRatio, ElevenLabsSpeechVoiceId, GoogleInteractionsAgentName) which are weaker enum candidates. AST-GREP RULES for a PreToolUse hook: (1) flag new String aliases — id: model-id-typealias-string / language: kotlin / rule: { pattern: 'typealias $N = String' }; (2) flag raw-String correlation ids — id: domain-id-raw-string / language: kotlin / rule: { pattern: 'val $N: String', constraints: { N: { regex: '(toolCallId|toolName|approvalId|providerId)$' } } }.
confirmed.dimension: String typealiases & primitive obsession

verdict.critique:
Evidence verified by reading QuiverAIProvider.kt end-to-end. Line 27 (typealias = String), line 86 (sentinel ?: "generate"), line 255 (binary if/else) all exist verbatim. The underlying pattern is genuine: "operation" is a closed two-value set {generate, vectorize} carried as a raw String pulled from a Map<String,JsonElement> with a sentinel default. So isReal = true.

However the finding contains two inaccuracies that the verifier must flag:

(1) DEAD TYPE. grep across .kt and .api shows `QuiverAIOperation` is referenced in exactly ONE place — its own declaration. Nothing uses it: the data class field is `operation: String? = null` (line 31), and every function parameter is `operation: String` (lines 87, 120, 124, 133, 254). It is an unused public typealias. This materially changes the ABI math below.

(2) OVERSTATED DANGER. The badPattern claims "a typo in 'generate' silently routes to vectorization." False. quiverAIRequestBody (lines 133-137) does an exhaustive when over {"generate","vectorize"} and throws InvalidArgumentError on anything else, and this runs at line 87 BEFORE quiverAIOperationPath is invoked at line 90. A typo throws; it does not silently route. The binary else at line 255 only ever sees a pre-validated "vectorize".

ABI ASSESSMENT IS WRONG (abiAssessmentCorrect=false). breaksAbi=true is incorrect. The builderTaskHint correctly says removing the typealias is not a break (it expands to String and is absent from the dumps — confirmed). It then justifies breaksAbi=true with "introducing the enum changes the public type that any signature using QuiverAIOperation exposes" — but NO public signature uses QuiverAIOperation. Therefore: removing the untracked alias = no break, AND adding a new public enum = additive. Net ABI impact is purely additive, NOT breaking. This is a self-contradiction in the finding's own reasoning.

solutionCorrect=true: the enum is idiomatic, compilable, removes (not merely supplements) the bad String type, and converts the path branch to an exhaustive when (T1). tenetAligned=true. Minor nit: the @SerialName annotations plus the manual wireValue property are redundant given operation arrives as an untyped JSON string from providerOptions and must be parsed at the boundary either way.

confirmed.title: providerOptions: Map<String, JsonElement> is the public request-side provider config across the entire generation surface (Record<string,unknown> preserved)
confirmed.tenet: T2 (separate wire from domain), T1 (sealed single source of truth), no-typed-Map<String,JsonElement>

Providers consume it via `providerOptions["anthropic"] as? JsonObject` then string-literal key lookups (`["cacheControl"]`, `["citations"]`, `["title"]`). There is no compile-time check that a key is valid, no discoverability of what a provider accepts, and a typo silently no-ops. This is the TS `Record<string, Record<string, unknown>>` translated 1:1.

The ABI dump api/jvm/torad-aisdk.api already freezes these signatures, so this MUST be additive: ship ProviderOptions sealed hierarchy + typed entries for the providers actually in-tree (anthropic, openai, google, bedrock), keep the old Map params as @Deprecated delegating to ProviderOptions.Raw. Do NOT silently change existing signatures. Pair the consumption-side rewrite (AnthropicProvider.kt:687-704 string-keyed casts) with the new types.
confirmed.dimension: Raw JSON / Map<String,JsonElement> leakage (Record<string,unknown>)

verdict.critique:
EVIDENCE CONFIRMED (read all four sites). LanguageModel.kt:82 `val providerOptions: Map<String, JsonElement> = emptyMap()` — verbatim. KotlinApi.kt:31 same on CallSettings (and CallSettingsBuilder.kt:45, ProviderOptionsBuilder at :88-109 is the string-keyed builder). AnthropicProvider.kt:688 `val canonical = providerOptions["anthropic"] as? JsonObject ?: JsonObject(emptyMap())` — verbatim. AnthropicProvider.kt:700-702 `(options["citations"] as? JsonObject)?.let{...}; options["title"]?.let{...}` — verbatim. The pattern is MORE pervasive than claimed: 63 declarations in commonMain and string-keyed consumption (`providerOptions["<name>"] as? JsonObject`) in ~30 of 35 provider files (Cohere:619, Xai:650, Google:235/288/365/550/1291, Bedrock:299/365/489, etc.). So isReal is unambiguous, and it genuinely violates T2 (wire leaks into public domain API) and the no-Map<String,JsonElement> tenet. The TS-residue claim is also accurate — this is `SharedV2ProviderOptions = Record<string, Record<string, JSONValue>>` translated 1:1.

WHY solutionCorrect=false (three concrete defects, not nitpicks):
1) UNDER-SCOPED TO THE POINT OF BEING A HALF-MEASURE: there are 35 provider files, ~30 consuming the raw bag. The proposal types only 4 (anthropic/openai/google/bedrock). The other ~26 (Cohere, Fal, Replicate, Deepgram, ElevenLabs, KlingAI, Luma, Prodia, AssemblyAI, Gladia, Hume, LMNT, Revai, HuggingFace, ByteDance, BlackForestLabs, QuiverAI, Alibaba, etc.) STILL funnel through `ProviderOptions.Raw` → `providerOptions["cohere"] as? JsonObject` → string-keyed lookups. So for ~87% of providers the bad pattern is not removed, just renamed. The verifier rubric explicitly rejects "add a good type while keeping the bad one"; as scoped, this keeps the bad path as the primary path for most providers.
2) SEALED-CLASS-IN-CORE CONFLICTS WITH T8 (feature-slice, no god files): a `sealed class ProviderOptions` is a CLOSED hierarchy — all subclasses must live in the same module/package as the base. Putting Anthropic/OpenAI/Google/Bedrock/Cohere/... variants in the core `LanguageModel` surface forces core to depend on every provider (reverse dependency, god file), or strands 31 providers on `Raw`. Sealed cannot be extended per-provider-slice, which is exactly what a 35-provider library needs.
3) `List<ProviderOptions>` is weaker than the Map it replaces: it loses key-dedup semantics (two `Anthropic` entries? a deprecated `Map`→`Raw("anthropic")` coexisting with a typed `Anthropic`? — merge ambiguity, unresolved by the parenthetical "ProviderOptionsSet"). It also conflates request-level providerOptions with per-PART metadata (AnthropicProvider.kt:694-703 reads `metadata["anthropic"]["cacheControl"]` on individual content parts — a separate axis the sealed request type doesn't cover).
Minor: `internal abstract fun toWireJson()` on a public sealed class compiles (subclasses are in-module) and is fine.

abiAssessmentCorrect=true: for a published explicitApi() KMP lib, this IS an ABI concern. `providerOptions` is frozen in api/jvm/torad-aisdk.api (line 82 field signatures, plus the `public final fun providerOptions(Function1)V` builder at :477). Adding a param to the `LanguageModelCallParams`/`CallSettings` data classes changes the synthetic constructor + copy()/componentN signatures — binary-incompatible — so breaksAbi=true is correct, and the builderTaskHint's insistence on additive + @Deprecated delegation is the right mitigation.

tenetAligned=true for the proposal's DIRECTION and explicit choices: sealed CLASS over interface (Marcos's stated preference), typed over raw, explicit Raw escape hatch instead of an implicit one, pre-computed toWireJson (T3). The defects above are scope/architecture problems, not tenet violations in the chosen primitives.
verdict.refinedSolution:
Keep the finding's GOAL (stop string-keying provider config; type it; honor T2) but change the mechanism to fit a 35-provider library and preserve feature-slicing (T8):

1) DO NOT model all providers as a sealed hierarchy in core. A sealed class is closed and cannot accept provider-slice-defined variants; enumerating 35 providers in core is a god file + reverse dependency. Instead, keep `providerOptions` keyed by the dynamic provider name (the name IS load-bearing — see AnthropicProvider.kt:689 `providerName.substringBefore('.')` and the customName merge at :690, plus HuggingFace:368 / OpenResponses:791 alias fallbacks), but make the VALUE typed via kotlinx.serialization.

2) Each provider slice defines its own `@Serializable data class AnthropicProviderOptions(val cacheControl: CacheControl? = null, val thinking: ThinkingConfig? = null, val citations: ..., val title: ..., val context: ...)` etc., living in that provider's file (feature-slice, no core coupling). The provider decodes its slice with `json.decodeFromJsonElement(AnthropicProviderOptions.serializer(), providerOptions["anthropic"] ?: return defaults)` instead of `as? JsonObject` + `options["title"]` string lookups. This removes the typo-silently-no-ops failure mode AND keeps the wire boundary in one serializer, not hand-rolled JsonObject building.

3) Public call-site typing via provider-slice extension builders, not a core sealed type:
   `public fun ProviderOptionsBuilder.anthropic(block: AnthropicProviderOptions.Builder.() -> Unit)` (or `anthropic(AnthropicProviderOptions(...))`) defined in the anthropic slice, encoding to the named JsonObject. Callers get compile-time-checked, discoverable options per provider; the core builder (KotlinApi.kt:88) stays provider-agnostic.

4) Keep an EXPLICIT raw escape hatch on the builder for not-yet-modeled providers/keys: `provider(name: String, JsonObject)` already exists (ProviderOptionsBuilder.provider at :103) — retain it as the documented boundary. This is the legitimate version of `Raw`, scoped to the long tail rather than being the primary path for 26 providers.

5) MIGRATION (additive; breaksAbi managed): do NOT mutate the `Map<String, JsonElement>` fields on the data classes (that breaks the frozen constructor/copy ABI). Add the typed builder extensions and provider-side typed decoders as PURELY ADDITIVE API; the existing `Map`-based `providerOptions`/`providerOption(name, JsonElement)` (KotlinApi.kt:61) stay as the underlying representation the extensions write into, so old call sites compile and run unchanged. Mark the bare `put(name, JsonElement)` / raw map mutation `@Deprecated(ReplaceWith(...))` pointing at the typed extensions for one cycle. Run kotlinx-binary-compatibility-validator (`apiCheck`) to confirm the .api dumps only GAIN lines.

Net: same wire format, same dynamic provider-name keying (required for `anthropic.bedrock`-style custom names and aliases), but typed/serializer-driven consumption and typed/discoverable construction, with providers self-contained in their slices — fixes all 35 providers' consumption pattern rather than 4, avoids the sealed-in-core god file, and stays ABI-additive.

The registry itself is correctly atomic/COW (AtomicReference CAS at Telemetry.kt:125-142, so no ConcurrentModificationException), but it is still global mutable state: behavior changes depending on whoever called registerTelemetry first, which is the test-isolation/hidden-dependency problem the tenet forbids.

BEFORE: `registerTelemetry(MyIntegration()) // affects whole process`. AFTER: `Agent(..., telemetry = TelemetrySettings(integrations = listOf(MyIntegration())))`.

Deprecating the three public global symbols is additive (keep them in api/*.api). Removing globalTelemetry/registerTelemetry/clearGlobalTelemetry is the only breaking step — gate it on a version bump and regenerate both api dumps. confirmed.badPattern: `gateway` is a process-wide singleton (Gateway.kt:254) backed by DefaultGatewayProvider, which caches fetch-metadata in two plain `var` fields (`pendingMetadata`, `lastFetchTime`, Gateway.kt:261-262) and reads-then-writes them inside the `suspend fun getAvailableModels()` (Gateway.kt:279-292) with no mutex/atomic. Concurrent coroutines hitting the shared singleton race on these vars (torn reads, lost writes, double-fetch). This is the same JS-pattern-carried-over class as openai (the default `gateway` val also throws because its default transport is GatewayTransportNotConfigured, Gateway.kt:33), but the cache race is a genuine correctness bug for any real transport, made process-wide by the shared val.
confirmed.solution: Two fixes: (a) drop the top-level `gateway` val (same treatment as GLOBAL-1 — keep only createGateway(settings)); (b) make the cache concurrency-safe — guard the read-modify-write with a Mutex, or model it as an `AtomicReference<CacheEntry?>` (mirroring TelemetryRegistry's CAS approach at Telemetry.kt:125) instead of two independent vars (which also kills the "exactly two correlated fields" smell, tenet T5). BEFORE: `private var pendingMetadata; private var lastFetchTime` mutated in a suspend fun. AFTER: `private val cache = AtomicReference<MetadataCache?>(null)` holding `data class MetadataCache(val response, val fetchedAtMillis)`, swapped atomically; or wrap the fetch in `mutex.withLock { ... }`. The cache fix is internal-only (ABI-safe); removing the `gateway` val is the breaking part.
confirmed.builderTaskHint: DefaultGatewayProvider is private, so making its cache atomic/mutex-guarded and collapsing the two vars into one AtomicReference<MetadataCache?> is purely internal — no api/*.api change, do this immediately. Separately deprecate then (version-gated) remove the public `gateway` val per GLOBAL-1's mechanism (that part regenerates the api dumps). Treat the cache race as the high-severity half; the singleton removal as the cleanup half.
confirmed.dimension: Mutable global/singleton state & JS default-export disguise

verdict.critique:
Evidence verified verbatim. Gateway.kt:254 `public val gateway: GatewayProvider = createGatewayProvider()` (a process-wide shared instance, confirmed in the ABI as getGateway()/klib `gateway`). DefaultGatewayProvider (private, line 256) holds two unsynchronized mutable fields: `private var pendingMetadata` (261) and `private var lastFetchTime: Long = Long.MIN_VALUE` (262). getAvailableModels (279-292) does an unguarded read-modify-write: reads `pendingMetadata`/`lastFetchTime`, then `transport.getAvailableModels(...).also { pendingMetadata = it; lastFetchTime = now }`. On multi-thread dispatchers (IO/Default) concurrent coroutines genuinely race here: no @Volatile/Mutex/atomic, the `Long` write isn't guaranteed atomic by the JLS, and there's a visibility gap. So the pattern is REAL.

Two corrections to the finding's framing:
1. SEVERITY is overstated as "high." The worst real-world outcome is a benign cache race: redundant double-fetches or briefly serving stale/uncached metadata — not data corruption of returned results (each call returns its own fetched `it`, not torn state). Medium is fairer.
2. The "made process-wide by the shared val" framing is weak. The race exists per-instance for ANY DefaultGatewayProvider with a configured transport, singleton or not. And the DEFAULT `gateway` singleton uses GatewayTransportNotConfigured (line 33/223), whose getAvailableModels THROWS before any write — so the default singleton never actually exercises the cache race. The singleton-removal half (GLOBAL-1 dup) is orthogonal cleanup, not the source of this bug.

SOLUTION: directionally correct and idiomatic. Collapsing the two correlated `var`s into one `data class MetadataCache(response, fetchedAtMillis)` swapped atomically genuinely satisfies T5 (kills the correlated-pair smell) and matches the existing codebase idiom (TelemetryRegistry uses kotlin.concurrent.atomics.AtomicReference with @OptIn(ExperimentalAtomicApi::class), .load()/.update(), confirmed Telemetry.kt:121-143). BUT an important nuance the finding glosses: AtomicReference alone does NOT prevent the double-fetch it lists as a symptom — two coroutines can both observe stale, both fetch, both store; CAS only guarantees no torn/lost-write, not single-flight. Only the Mutex.withLock option (also offered) serializes the read-modify-write to dedupe fetches. So if double-fetch suppression matters, Mutex (or an in-flight Deferred) is required; AtomicReference fixes visibility/atomicity only.

ABI: marked false, which is wrong for the COMPOSITE solution. The cache fix alone is ABI-safe (DefaultGatewayProvider is private — true). But the solution explicitly also removes the public `gateway` val, which IS in the tracked ABI (api/jvm:1730 getGateway(), klib:13968) — removing it is a hard binary break requiring @Deprecated migration, not breaksAbi:false. The finding's own prose admits "removing the gateway val is the breaking part," contradicting its headline boolean. Hence abiAssessmentCorrect=false.

confirmed.badPattern: A public `suspend fun` that drives a Flow via `.collect { }` and wraps the whole body in `catch (t: Throwable)` that converts the error into mutable `error` state + `options.onError(t)`. id: catch-throwable-swallows-cancellation
language: kotlin
rule:
  kind: catch_block

confirmed.builderTaskHint: Two-line edit in src/commonMain/kotlin/ai/torad/aisdk/StructuredObjectApi.kt: (1) add `import kotlinx.coroutines.CancellationException`, (2) insert `} catch (c: CancellationException) { throw c` between the AbortError catch (line 114) and the Throwable catch (line 116). confirmed.dimension: Coroutines/Flow correctness: cancellation, buffering, suspend-vs-Flow, backpressure

verdict.critique: Finding confirmed by reading the files. StructuredObjectApi.kt:114-118 wraps a Flow-collecting suspend body in `catch (abort: AbortError)` followed by a bare `catch (t: Throwable) { error = t; options.onError(t) }` with no CancellationException rethrow. Critically, AbortSignal.kt:124 defines `AbortError : kotlin.coroutines.cancellation.CancellationException`, so the AbortError clause only intercepts the abort-specific subtype; a plain cooperative CancellationException (parent scope/coroutine cancellation, not an explicit abort()) is not an AbortError and therefore falls through to `catch (t: Throwable)`, where it is swallowed into mutable `error` state and reported via `onError`. That converts structured-concurrency cancellation into a fake user-visible error — a real violation of coroutine cancellation cooperation (T4). The sibling CompletionApi.kt:89-93 already carries the fix (`catch (t: CancellationException) { throw t }` between the AbortError and Throwable clauses), and the CancellationException import is indeed absent from StructuredObjectApi.kt:3-5, so the fix genuinely needs the import added. The catch ordering in the solution is correct: AbortError (most specific) first preserves the partial-state-on-abort behavior, the generic CancellationException rethrow next preserves cancellation, Throwable last for real errors. The `finally` block correctly remains and still runs on cancellation. ABI assessment is right — adding a catch clause inside a function body changes no public signature; breaksAbi:false. Not a half-measure: it removes the swallow rather than layering a new path on top of it.

confirmed.badPattern: OLD (committed state the report reviewed): `coroutineScope { toolsToExecute.map { async { ... BufferingCollector() ... } }.awaitAll() }` then replay each tool's buffered events serially. confirmed.solution: NO ACTION — the rewrite is correct. Children run under a bounded Semaphore and push both progress (StreamEvent) and final results onto one parent-owned `Channel`; only the parent collector coroutine performs real `emit`/`applyToolResult`, preserving the Flow single-collector invariant on Kotlin/Native. Progress now streams incrementally (no whole-stream buffering), durable results are applied in original call order via `nextToApply`, and because the parent's `parentOut.emit` suspends on slow downstream collectors the channel fills and children block on `send` — i.e. backpressure is correctly restored end-to-end. A child exception propagates through `coroutineScope` (cancels siblings), so no hang. The bug from report claim #9 is fixed in the working tree.

verdict.critique:
VERIFIED by reading the file and the ABI files. The evidence is accurate, not fabricated: `wc -l` = 1505 lines, and 10 distinct `public interface X : Provider` declarations exist at lines 61 (DeepSeek), 89 (Cerebras), 115 (DeepInfra), 169 (Fireworks), 191 (Perplexity), 219 (MoonshotAI), 253 (Groq), 317 (TogetherAI), 343 (Vercel), 376 (Baseten). Each provider carries its own Settings/Options/ErrorData data classes, typealiases, createX factory, default `val`, and a private Default*Provider impl class (lines 405-552+), all in this single file. The cited snippets at line 61, 89, 169, and 72 all say what is claimed. This is a genuine T8 god-file (ten independent third-party integrations sharing one file). Severity medium is fair.

ABI assessment is correct (breaksAbi=false IS achievable) but ONLY under the constraints the solution itself names, and the solution contains one self-contradiction the builder must heed: api/jvm/torad-aisdk.api line 11223 shows the JVM facade class `OpenAICompatibleProviderFacadesKt` holding static `createDeepSeek`/`getDeepseek`; api/torad-aisdk.klib.api places the types (DeepSeekProvider line 491, DeepSeekProviderSettings line 5388) in package `ai.torad.aisdk.providers`. Therefore: (a) the top-level funcs/vals require `@file:JvmName("OpenAICompatibleProviderFacadesKt")` + `@file:JvmMultifileClass` on every split file to preserve the JVM facade — the solution states this correctly; (b) the types are ABI-free to move ONLY if they stay in package `ai.torad.aisdk.providers`. The solution's parenthetical "providers/openaicompatible/" is a TRAP: if those files declare `package ai.torad.aisdk.providers.openaicompatible`, every type's fully-qualified name changes, breaking BOTH the klib ABI and the JVM ABI. The "(or providers/)" alternative is the only ABI-safe one. The fix is a full split (not a half-measure) and is tenet-aligned with T8 and the library's additive/no-silent-break mandate.

Note on a deeper anti-pattern outside this dimension's scope: the 10 `public val deepseek = providerNotConfigured(...)` throwing singletons (lines 72,100,128,182,201,229,283,331,353) are exactly the "magic top-level vals that throw at runtime" the tenets forbid in favor of explicit construction/DI. The split does not fix that and should not pretend to; flag it as a separate finding.
verdict.refinedSolution:
Split into one file per provider, KEEPING the package `ai.torad.aisdk.providers` (do NOT introduce a `.openaicompatible` subpackage — that breaks both klib and JVM ABI). Place files directly under `src/commonMain/kotlin/ai/torad/aisdk/providers/` as DeepSeek.kt, Cerebras.kt, DeepInfra.kt, Fireworks.kt, Perplexity.kt, MoonshotAI.kt, Groq.kt, TogetherAI.kt, Vercel.kt, Baseten.kt. Each holds that provider's typealiases + Settings/Options/ErrorData data classes + Provider interface + createX fun + default val + Default*Provider impl + any Tools object + its VERSION const.

ABI preservation (acceptance = `checkKotlinAbi` zero diff):
1. Every new file that contains top-level funcs/vals (createX, the default vals, VERSION consts) MUST start with:
   `@file:JvmMultifileClass`
   `@file:JvmName("OpenAICompatibleProviderFacadesKt")`
   before the package line. This keeps all static members in the existing JVM facade class (api/jvm line 11223). A multifile facade emits a facade + part classes, but the public signatures `OpenAICompatibleProviderFacadesKt.createDeepSeek(...)`/`getDeepseek()` are byte-identical, so binary-compatibility-validator shows no diff.
2. Types stay in `package ai.torad.aisdk.providers` — klib unaffected.
3. Move `providerNotConfigured` (and any other shared private/inline helpers, line 968) to a small `FacadeShared.kt`. Because it is `private inline` it can stay `internal`/`private` per-file or be made `internal` in the shared file; if it stays `private inline`, it must be duplicated or promoted to `internal` since callers are now in different files — promote to `internal` in FacadeShared.kt to avoid duplication.

Pure mechanical move, no logic changes. After splitting, run `./gradlew apiCheck`/`checkKotlinAbi` to confirm zero diff on both api/jvm/torad-aisdk.api and api/torad-aisdk.klib.api.

Separately (do not bundle into this T8 split, file as its own finding): the 10 throwing default `val` singletons (`providerNotConfigured(...)`) violate the no-magic-top-level-vals-that-throw tenet; the intended migration is explicit construction/DI via the existing createX factories, with the throwing vals `@Deprecated` for additive removal.

OpenResponsesProvider.kt (1118 lines): facade + wire + stream parser whose accept() at ~:931 maintains PendingOpenResponsesToolCall state in toolCallsByItemId (~:944). HuggingFaceProvider.kt is 762 lines. The provider-barrel fingerprint is real and repeats across all four — this is a genuine T8 (god-file/feature-slice) and T2 (wire-vs-domain co-location) violation, aggravated for Bedrock because security-sensitive SigV4 signing and untrusted-input binary parsing sit in the same file as routine option mapping.

ABI assessment is correct and I verified the load-bearing claim: api/jvm/torad-aisdk.api:7789 shows `AmazonBedrockProviderKt` as the JVM facade class holding the static createAmazonBedrock/createBedrockAnthropic/createBedrockMantle funcs and getBedrock/AMAZON_BEDROCK_VERSION accessors; identical facade classes exist for Anthropic/HuggingFace/OpenResponses. The solution explicitly prescribes that recipe, so breaksAbi:false is accurate (private model/wire/stream classes never appear in the ABI; public classes/interfaces/data classes move freely by package; klib is package-addressed and unaffected). The gate on `./gradlew checkKotlinAbi` ZERO diff is the right safety net.

Solution is idiomatic and genuinely better, not a half-measure: it is a pure structural decomposition that changes no types, no invariants, no Flow/suspend contracts — it relocates the existing accept()-based stream state machines and wire mappers into feature-slice files. Minor caveats, not blockers: (1) effort L for a 'medium' severity org concern — the real payoff is Bedrock's EventStream.kt + Auth.kt isolation for security review, which should be prioritized and could ship independently of the lower-value Anthropic/OpenResponses/HuggingFace splits; (2) the solution leaves pre-existing T5 smells untouched (e.g. .orEmpty() sentinel fallback on Smithy header fields at :1328-1330, and several `?: ""`/orEmpty() on item ids in the OpenResponses parser) — out of scope here but worth flagging as adjacent cleanup when EventStream.kt/Stream.kt are extracted.

confirmed.dimension: Loose logic vs legitimate functional API; companion overuse

verdict.critique:
Verified all three cited file:line anchors directly. GoogleProvider.kt:1284 = `private fun googleGenerateContentBody(...)`; OpenAICompatibleProviderFacades.kt:728 = `private fun deepSeekTransformChatBody(body: JsonObject): JsonObject`; AnthropicProvider.kt:283 = `private fun anthropicRequestBody(...)`. All exact.

Counts: claimed per-file numbers are slightly inflated but the right order of magnitude. Actual top-level `private fun` counts: GoogleProvider 44 (claim 50), OpenAICompatibleProviderFacades 43 (47), AmazonBedrockProvider 40 (44), OpenResponsesProvider 40 (40), AnthropicProvider 28 (28). Total private top-level functions across providers/*.kt ~492 (claim 556 — likely counts internal too, or a looser AST query). The discrepancy does not change the finding: this is pervasive and real.

Pattern confirmed in substance, not just count: GoogleProvider has a dense family of `google*`/`googleInteractions*` file-scope functions (googleGenerateContentBody, googleMessages, googleContentPart, googleInteractionsUsage, googleInteractionsFinishReason, ...) operating on raw JsonObject/JsonElement (202 JsonObject/JsonElement mentions in the file). The prefix is a stand-in for a receiver type. T7 hard-to-test smell is real: the model classes that call these (GoogleGenerativeAILanguageModel etc.) are themselves `private`, and the factory returns a public Provider interface — so the wire functions are reachable from tests ONLY by driving a full HTTP call. grep of src/commonTest for deepSeekTransformChatBody/googleMessages/googleGenerateContentBody/anthropicRequestBody = 0 references. Confirmed.

ABI assessment correct: the klib/jvm api files expose only public surface (createGoogleGenerativeAI factory, GoogleGenerativeAIProvider, google/googleTools vals, GoogleTools.googleSearch). The 10 'google' klib hits are all public types/factories — NONE are the private wire functions. Moving private free functions into private/internal codec types is invisible to ABI. breaksAbi:false is right.

Solution: the mandatory core — collapse the providerXxx free-function family into an owned, testable unit (internal class with settings, or internal object when stateless) with the helpers as private methods, plus a per-provider unit test asserting mapping with no network — is idiomatic, compilable, and a genuine fix (not a half-measure: it removes the free functions rather than wrapping them). It preserves the Flow contract (model classes already `override fun stream(): Flow<StreamEvent>`; internal SSE accumulators return List<StreamEvent> and stay). No new nullable-pair/sentinel/mutable-global introduced.

The one over-reach is the proposed shared `internal interface WireCodec { requestBody; parseResult; streamEvents }` spanning ALL providers. Providers are structurally heterogeneous — Google alone has language, embedding, image, video, AND an Interactions API model; Bedrock/OpenResponses differ again. Forcing one common WireCodec interface risks speculative abstraction (T6: speculative complexity bad) and a lowest-common-denominator shape that fights the per-provider reality. The solution hedges this ('e.g.', 'or internal object'), so I accept it, but the shared interface should be DROPPED from the mandatory scope and adopted only if a real second implementor needs it.
verdict.refinedSolution:
Per provider, collapse the `providerXxx` free-function family into one owned, testable type — NO cross-provider shared interface (that is speculative; add it only if a concrete second consumer appears). Stateless providers: `internal object GoogleWireCodec`. Stateful (needs settings/modelId): `internal class GoogleWireCodec(private val settings: GoogleGenerativeAIProviderSettings)`. Move `googleGenerateContentBody`, `googleMessages`, `googleContentPart`, `googleUsage`, `google*FinishReason`, etc. in as private/internal methods of that type; the existing private model classes (GoogleGenerativeAILanguageModel ...) delegate to it.

Surface only what tests need as `internal`:
- `internal fun requestBody(params: LanguageModelCallParams, stream: Boolean): GooglePreparedRequest`
- `internal fun parseResult(json: JsonObject): LanguageModelResult`
- streaming stays a `Flow<StreamEvent>` at the model boundary (`override fun stream(...): Flow<StreamEvent>`); the incremental SSE accumulator can remain a private method returning `List<StreamEvent>` per chunk — do not change that contract.

Add `GoogleWireCodecTest` in commonTest exercising requestBody/parseResult against fixture JSON with zero network. This directly retires the T7 'only testable via HTTP' smell.

Scope/sequencing: one provider per PR, start with GoogleProvider.kt (44 funcs, 2044-line god file). All moved functions are `private`/`internal` → invisible to api/*.api and the klib ABI; do NOT touch the public `createGoogleGenerativeAI` factory, the `google`/`googleTools` vals, or public model/provider interfaces. Run checkKotlinAbi after each PR to confirm zero diff.

Do NOT introduce typed domain models for the wire JSON as part of this change — the wire layer legitimately speaks JsonObject (kotlinx.serialization). The win here is encapsulation + testability (T7/T8), not a T2 retype; keep the diff surgical.

Anthropic/Fal/Bedrock bodies are byte-identical (`fields.forEach { (k,v) -> if (v !is JsonNull) put(k,v) }`); Google's adds an `excluded: Set<String> = emptySet()` param and is a strict superset. BUT the finding's second pillar — "19 near-identical `*Usage(JsonElement?): Usage` parsers each re-implement extraction" — is OVERSTATED, and this is what makes the SOLUTION wrong. Reading the bodies: `anthropicUsage` (AnthropicProvider.kt:1039) sums an `iterations` JsonArray and handles cache_creation/cache_read keys; `googleUsage` (GoogleProvider.kt:1892) reads camelCase wire keys (promptTokenCount/candidatesTokenCount/thoughtsTokenCount/cachedContentTokenCount) with output total = candidates+thoughts; the OpenAI-compatible cluster (deepSeek/perplexity/moonshot/groq/togetherAI in OpenAICompatibleProviderFacades.kt) reads snake_case and ALREADY shares `usageFromParts`/`intField`/`nestedIntField` (the right amount of DRY is already present).
