The solution even concedes "providers that genuinely differ keep an override" — that escape hatch is the tell that the abstraction leaks. Bundling a correct dedup (putJsonObjectFields) with an over-reach (Usage collapse) makes solutionCorrect = false.

tenetAligned = false: the putJsonObjectFields promotion aligns with T7/T8 (one owned internal unit), but the UsageFieldMapping collapse pushes against T2 by merging distinct per-provider wire transforms into a shared mapping table with special-case overrides.

abiAssessmentCorrect = true: breaksAbi:false is right. All helpers are `private`; promoting to `internal` is ABI-neutral because api/torad-aisdk.klib.api and api/jvm/torad-aisdk.api track only public/protected surface. No public signature changes.

Split the finding into the real win and drop the over-reach.

1) DO the putJsonObjectFields dedup (genuine, cheap, ABI-neutral). Create `src/commonMain/kotlin/ai/torad/aisdk/providers/WireJson.kt`:
```kotlin
internal fun JsonObjectBuilder.putJsonObjectFields(
    fields: JsonObject,
    excluded: Set<String> = emptySet(),
) = fields.forEach { (key, value) ->
    if (value !is JsonNull && key !in excluded) put(key, value)
}
```
Delete the 4 `private` copies (Anthropic:1145, Fal:413, Bedrock:1578, Google:2039). All providers share package `ai.torad.aisdk.providers`, so `internal` is sufficient — no import churn, no public ABI delta. Add one shared test asserting JsonNull-drop and `excluded` filtering. The 3 existing call-sites that pass no `excluded` keep working (default param).

2) DO NOT collapse the 19 `*Usage` parsers behind a `UsageFieldMapping`. They are legitimately distinct provider wire formats (T2). The OpenAI-compatible snake_case cluster is ALREADY factored via `usageFromParts`/`intField`/`nestedIntField`; the only sound additional move is to LIFT those three already-shared helpers (currently in OpenAICompatibleProviderFacades.kt) into WireJson.kt as `internal`, so other snake_case OpenAI-shaped providers can reuse them — while each provider keeps its own key-selection body. Leave `anthropicUsage` (iterations sum) and `googleUsage` (camelCase, unclamped output) exactly as they are; forcing them into a mapping table trades a clear per-provider transform for a leaky generic with override escapes. Net: one real dedup (putJsonObjectFields), one optional helper lift, zero false abstractions, breaksAbi:false confirmed.

confirmed.title: Cohesive algorithms in Util.kt live as private top-level free functions instead of owned, directly-testable units
confirmed.tenet: T7 (a hard-to-test private function points at a unit not yet extracted), T3

evidence.snippet: private fun classifyRetryFailure(t, policy, shouldRetry, errors, exponentialBackoffDelay): Long { ... }  // + retryDelayMs(221) + retryAfterDelayMs(243): whole retry decision, private, only reachable via retryWithExponentialBackoff

public fun validateDownloadUrl(url: String) { ... }  // SSRF policy via private parseUrl(487)/isIPv4(502)/isPrivateIPv4(510)/isPrivateIPv6(522) — security branch logic only exercised through the throwing public entrypoint

confirmed.badPattern: Two self-contained algorithms — retry/backoff decision (classifyRetryFailure/retryDelayMs/retryAfterDelayMs) and the SSRF URL-safety policy (validateDownloadUrl + parseUrl/isIPv4/isPrivateIPv4/isPrivateIPv6) — are scattered as private top-level functions in the 700-line Util.kt grab-bag. The decision logic (which IP ranges are private, whether a Retry-After is honored) is precisely the pre-computed decision (T3) that deserves an owned, exhaustively unit-testable type; as private free functions the branches are testable only by side effect (throw / delay).
confirmed.solution: Extract each cluster into a small owned unit that returns a value instead of branching/throwing inline. SSRF BEFORE: `private fun isPrivateIPv4(ip): Boolean` (untestable). AFTER: `internal object UrlSafetyPolicy { fun evaluate(url): UrlVerdict }` with `sealed class UrlVerdict { object Allowed; data class Blocked(val reason: BlockReason) }`; `validateDownloadUrl` becomes a thin `when(UrlSafetyPolicy.evaluate(url)){...throw}` shim, every IP-range branch directly testable. Same for retry: `internal object RetryDecision { fun next(t, policy, attempts): RetryStep }` with `sealed class RetryStep { data class Wait(val ms: Long); data class Fail(val error: Throwable) }` (T1 sealed result, T3 caller reacts to data). Keep retryWithExponentialBackoff/validateDownloadUrl as public functional API.
confirmed.builderTaskHint: Extracted pieces stay internal — public signatures unchanged, zero ABI impact. Lower priority than LL-01/LL-02 because public entrypoints already have boundary tests (GatewayAndProviderUtilsParityTest.kt:240-244 covers validateDownloadUrl); win is making internal branches (private IPv6 ranges, Retry-After clamping) directly assertable. Do NOT mass-split Util.kt — most of its 51 functions (base64, header merge, cosineSimilarity, id-gen) are legitimately stateless utils; only extract the two cohesive algorithms named here.
confirmed.dimension: Loose logic vs legitimate functional API; companion overuse

verdict.critique: Evidence is accurate. I opened Util.kt and confirmed every cited symbol at the stated lines: classifyRetryFailure (176), retryDelayMs (221), retryAfterDelayMs (243) form the entire retry decision as private top-level functions reachable only via retryWithExponentialBackoff; validateDownloadUrl (464) throws inline while parseUrl (487), isIPv4 (502), isPrivateIPv4 (510), isPrivateIPv6 (522) implement the SSRF policy as private free functions. The pattern is exactly as described, and the internal decision branches (the four retry throw paths, the 60s Retry-After clamp at MAX_RETRY_AFTER_MS, the IPv6 fc/fd/fe80/::ffff: ranges) are only exercisable through side effects (throw/delay) because they are private in commonMain. This genuinely engages T7 (hard-to-test private function = unextracted unit) and T3 (pre-computed decision deserving a value type). The proposed fix is a complete extraction (public shims kept, bad private form removed) not a half-measure, uses sealed CLASS results (UrlVerdict, RetryStep) per T1, introduces no nullable-pair or sentinel, and keeps everything internal so the klib/jvm ABI in api/*.api is unaffected — breaksAbi=false is correct. The one defect: RetryDecision.next(t, policy, attempts) as written cannot rebuild RetryError because it loses the accumulated errors list that classifyRetryFailure mutates; the extracted unit must thread the errors list through. Caveat on value: validateDownloadUrl is a validation function where throwing is a legitimate contract, so the verdict-then-shim adds some ceremony; the win is testability of the IP-range branches, which is real but modest — the finding's own medium/low-priority framing is honest.
verdict.refinedSolution:
Extract two internal, pure, directly-testable units; keep the public entrypoints as thin shims (zero ABI impact).

SSRF: internal object UrlSafetyPolicy { fun evaluate(url: String): UrlVerdict } with sealed class UrlVerdict { object Allowed : UrlVerdict(); data class Blocked(val reason: BlockReason) : UrlVerdict() } and an exhaustive sealed class BlockReason (InvalidUrl, BadScheme(scheme), MissingHost, LoopbackHost(host), PrivateIPv4(ip), PrivateIPv6(host)). Move parseUrl/isIPv4/isPrivateIPv4/isPrivateIPv6 into the object as private members. validateDownloadUrl becomes: when (val v = UrlSafetyPolicy.evaluate(url)) { UrlVerdict.Allowed -> Unit; is UrlVerdict.Blocked -> throw DownloadError(url, message(v.reason)) } so the exact DownloadError strings stay identical (parity tests unchanged) while every IP-range branch is assertable as a returned BlockReason.

Retry: internal object RetryDecision { fun next(t: Throwable, policy: RetryPolicy, priorErrors: List<Throwable>, exponentialBackoffDelay: Long): RetryStep } with sealed class RetryStep { data class Wait(val ms: Long) : RetryStep(); data class Fail(val error: Throwable) : RetryStep() }. Critically, thread priorErrors in (the original mutates the errors list and builds RetryError(errors.toList())) — without it the unit cannot reconstruct the RetryError payload; have next() compute errors = priorErrors + t internally and return Fail(RetryError(..., errors)) or Fail(t) for the unwrapped first-try/maxRetries==0 paths, preserving all four terminal behaviors. retryWithExponentialBackoff keeps the accumulating MutableList and does: when (val step = RetryDecision.next(t, policy, errors, nextDelay)) { is RetryStep.Wait -> { errors += t; delay(step.ms) }; is RetryStep.Fail -> throw step.error }. retryAfterDelayMs/retryDelayMs become private helpers of RetryDecision and are now unit-testable on the RetryStep value. Both objects are internal, so api/jvm/torad-aisdk.api and api/torad-aisdk.klib.api are unchanged. Do NOT touch the other ~49 stateless utils in Util.kt (base64, header merge, cosineSimilarity, id-gen) — they are legitimately stateless and out of scope.

confirmed.title: ToolLoopAgent's 30+-knob flattened primary constructor is frozen into the ABI — every future upstream knob breaks binary compat
confirmed.tenet: T6 (cheap structural future-proofing) / library binary-compat

evidence.snippet: public val experimental_toolApprovalSecret: ByteArray? = null,  // a knob added later — proof the param list grows over time

public fun <init> (Lai/torad/aisdk/LanguageModel;Ljava/lang/String;Lai/torad/aisdk/ToolSet;Ljava/util/List;Lai/torad/aisdk/Output;Lai/torad/aisdk/StopCondition;Lkotlin/jvm/functions/Function2; ... ;Lkotlin/coroutines/CoroutineContext;)V  // single 30-arg ctor + synthetic II-mask DefaultConstructorMarker variant frozen as the 1.0 contract

confirmed.badPattern: A published, extend-me abstract class whose entire configuration surface is one flat 30-parameter constructor with default args. On JVM this compiles to ONE <init> descriptor plus a synthetic DefaultConstructorMarker variant carrying bitmask Ints; on klib it is one constructor signature. Both are frozen in api/*.api. Adding the 31st knob (the SDK demonstrably tracks upstream and keeps adding knobs — experimental_toolApprovalSecret is one such late addition) changes the descriptor and the synthetic mask arity, a hard binary-incompatible break for every compiled subclass and caller. The flat list also has no cohesion: sampler params, lifecycle hooks, telemetry, security, and engine wiring are all peers.
confirmed.builderTaskHint: Introduce grouped config data classes (AgentSamplerDefaults, AgentHooks, AgentEngineConfig) and make them the primary constructor params. Keep the current 30-arg constructor as a @Deprecated(level = HIDDEN) secondary that delegates, so the existing api/*.api symbol is preserved and checkKotlinAbi only sees additions. Regenerate both api/torad-aisdk.klib.api and api/jvm/torad-aisdk.api and confirm the old <init> line still exists. This MUST land before 1.0 freeze — after freeze the flat ctor can never absorb a new knob without a major-version break.
confirmed.dimension: KMP library API hygiene & binary compatibility

verdict.critique:
Evidence fully verified by reading the files. ToolLoopAgent.kt:60-143 is a 33-arg primary constructor on a public abstract (extend-me) class; the late experimental_toolApprovalSecret param (line 118, doc-cited to upstream v6.0.202) genuinely proves the list grows over time; api/jvm/torad-aisdk.api:6738-6739 confirms the single real <init> + synthetic II-mask DefaultConstructorMarker variant; KotlinApi.kt:21 confirms CallSettings is already a data-class config bag, so the solution's analogy is sound.

ABI assessment is correct. For a Kotlin abstract class with all-default constructor params, a subclass's super(...) call compiles against the synthetic <init>(...IILDefaultConstructorMarker)V symbol. Appending any param (even with a default) changes that descriptor, so already-compiled subclasses and Java/direct callers hit NoSuchMethodError. breaksAbi=true is right, and BCV tracks it in api/*.api.

The core solution (group knobs into AgentSamplerDefaults / AgentHooks / AgentEngineConfig and pass a handful of stable config params) is idiomatic, compilable, mirrors the existing CallSettings pattern, and is NOT a half-measure — it removes the flat constructor rather than adding alongside it. The real, high-value win is that the abstract class's super-constructor contract stops churning; new fields land inside a config data class instead.

Two caveats that keep this from being flawless: (1) The proposed migration — "keep the old 30-arg constructor as @Deprecated(HIDDEN) secondary to preserve the ABI symbol" — is over-stated. A hand-written secondary cannot reproduce the synthetic $default(...IILDefaultConstructorMarker) symbol that existing Kotlin subclasses actually reference, so it would not fully preserve ABI for compiled subclasses. (2) The framing "frozen as the 1.0 contract" is slightly hyperbolic: the module is 0.3.0-alpha01, so breaks are currently cheap. That actually strengthens the recommendation to restructure NOW, before 1.0 freeze, rather than weakening it.

Tenet check: data classes for config aggregates is consistent with the codebase's own CallSettings (also a data class); no sealed-hierarchy is warranted here, no nullable-pair invariant, no sentinel fabrication introduced. Note the proposed AgentSamplerDefaults uses responseFormat: ResponseFormat = ResponseFormat.Text (non-null), which carries the same sentinel-vs-explicit ambiguity CallSettings deliberately avoided by making responseFormat nullable (KotlinApi.kt:26-28) — but this only preserves the existing constructor's current behavior (line 85), so it is not a new violation.

confirmed.dimension: KMP library API hygiene & binary compatibility

verdict.critique: Evidence confirmed by reading both files: Generate.kt:20/57/196/336/447/517 hold the flat ~15-param families and KotlinApi.kt:21 + 301/325 hold the CallSettings family. The two parallel public/frozen surfaces genuinely coexist, so isReal=true. ABI assessment is also correct: @Deprecated is annotation-only, ABI-safe, and the .api dump changes only deprecation metadata without breaking caller linkage.\n\nBut the SOLUTION direction is wrong/half-baked: (1) The flat named-default-param overload IS idiomatic Kotlin (named args are the Kotlin answer to long lists) and is the delegation BASE — every CallSettings/request/block overload in KotlinApi.kt (lines 311-323, 391-407) calls INTO the flat generateText/streamTextResult. Deprecating the base inverts the dependency and produces internal deprecation noise. (3) ReplaceWith(\"generateText(model, CallSettings(...))\") is not a valid concrete expression; the `...` makes the quick-fix non-functional. Specifically, the flat+settings overloads (301, 325) and the request-wrapping overloads (258, 278) and the settings+block DSL (269, 291) are three near-identical ways to pass the same grouped data. Pick ONE grouped entry (the `TextGenerationRequest` request-object form, which already composes prompt/messages/system/settings) as canonical, and @Deprecate the redundant grouped convenience overloads — not the flat primitive. If any deprecation is added, use a CONCRETE ReplaceWith (e.g. the request-object call), never `CallSettings(...)` with an ellipsis, since that is not a valid expression and yields no quick-fix. Do not re-deprecate generateObject/streamObject (already @Deprecated). All of this is ABI-safe (annotation-only); run checkKotlinAbi to confirm api/*.api changes are deprecation metadata only. confirmed.title: 85 provider model-id types are `typealias X = String` instead of value classes — type-unsafe public API the codebase already knows how to fix
confirmed.tenet: value classes over `typealias X = String`; make illegal states unrepresentable

evidence.snippet:

public typealias GatewayEmbeddingModelId = String
public typealias GatewayImageModelId = String

public typealias XaiChatModelId = String
public typealias XaiResponsesModelId = String

public value class ModelId(public val value: String) { init { require(value.isNotBlank()) ... } }  // the codebase ALREADY has the right pattern — just not applied to provider ids

confirmed.badPattern: Public API model identifiers declared as `typealias SomethingModelId = String` (85 occurrences across providers + Gateway). A typealias gives zero type safety (any String is assignable, no validation, providers' ids are interchangeable) and provides no migration anchor. The codebase already ships @JvmInline value class ModelId/ProviderId in ModelRef.kt with blank-checks — proving the team's own standard contradicts these aliases. On JVM these aliases erase to String and don't even appear in api/jvm (confirmed: grep for XaiChatModelId in the JVM dump = 0 hits), so they buy nothing at the ABI layer either.
confirmed.builderTaskHint: Convert provider model-id typealiases to @JvmInline value classes with a non-blank require, reusing the ModelRef.kt pattern. This changes klib descriptors and (for functions taking them by value) JVM descriptors — must land before 1.0 freeze. Regenerate both api dumps. If time-boxed, prioritize ids that actually appear in public function signatures; pure-documentation aliases can be deleted instead.
confirmed.dimension: KMP library API hygiene & binary compatibility

verdict.critique: Evidence fully confirmed by reading the files. There are exactly 85 `public typealias X = String` declarations (Gateway.kt:9-13, XaiProvider.kt:32-35, and across ~30 provider files). ModelRef.kt:5-22 confirms the codebase already ships `@JvmInline value class ProviderId/ModelId` with `require(value.isNotBlank())`, so the team's own standard does contradict these aliases. The aliases are not merely documentation: they appear in real public signatures (XaiProvider.kt:88-92 `chat(modelId: XaiChatModelId)`, Gateway.kt:172/234/311, AnthropicProvider.kt:56-58, GoogleVertexProvider.kt:118-120). ABI assessment is correct: grep of the JVM dump for `XaiChatModelId` = 0 hits and the functions erase to `(Ljava/lang/String;)`; converting to inline value classes changes JVM descriptors (inline-class parameters trigger method-name mangling) and klib types, so breaksAbi=true. Tenet alignment is direct — Marcos's explicit rule is "value classes or sealed types over `typealias X = String`"; no Flow/sealed/nullable-pair concerns apply here. The one weakness is in the SHAPE of the proposed fix, not its correctness: minting 85 distinct per-provider value classes is speculative complexity (brushes T6) and imposes a real DX regression. Today `xai("grok-3")` accepts a bare literal; a per-provider value class forces `chat(XaiChatModelId("grok-3"))` at every call site, which is more ceremony than the TS original it ports (TS used `'grok-3' | (string & {})` union literals for autocomplete — not reproducible in Kotlin, and a value class doesn't restore it). So the type-safety win (you can't cross provider ids) is mostly theoretical for string-literal usage. The solution does NOT keep the bad pattern (so it's not a rejected half-measure) and it compiles, hence solutionCorrect=true — but the finding's own fallback ("route through the existing ModelId value class" / "delete aliases never used in a public signature") is the better landing.
verdict.refinedSolution:
Do not mint 85 new value classes. Two-track fix that preserves ergonomics and binary-compat discipline:

1. Pure-noise aliases (no public-signature use) — DELETE them outright. e.g. XaiResponsesModelId/XaiImageModelId/XaiVideoModelId, the Bedrock/Google/Fireworks/TogetherAI families etc. that only annotate parameters whose JVM/klib type is already String. Removing a typealias that never appears in the ABI dump is a SOURCE-only change with zero binary impact (verified: these are absent from api/jvm/*.api). This eliminates the dead type noise the tenet objects to, at no ABI cost.

2. Aliases used in public signatures (XaiChatModelId, AnthropicMessagesModelId, GatewayModelId, CohereChatModelId, ...) — converge on the SINGLE existing `ModelId` value class from ModelRef.kt rather than per-provider clones. Change the typed entry points to take `ModelId`, and ADD String-accepting overloads so `xai("grok-3")` still works:
   `public fun chat(modelId: ModelId): LanguageModel`
   `public fun chat(modelId: String): LanguageModel = chat(ModelId(modelId))`
   This restores the non-blank guarantee, gives one coherent identifier type across the SDK, and keeps the literal call site ergonomic.

ABI handling (published, explicitApi, breaksAbi=true): land pre-1.0 in one sweep. The String overload keeps the old `(Ljava/lang/String;)` JVM descriptor alive (additive), while the new `ModelId` overload adds the inline-class entry point (mangled name on JVM, value-class type in klib). Regenerate BOTH api/jvm/torad-aisdk.api and api/torad-aisdk.klib.api and review the diff. If even the additive overloads are deemed too much churn before freeze, the minimum viable step is track 1 alone (delete the unused aliases) plus a follow-up issue for track 2 — never expand to 85 bespoke value classes, which buys little type safety for string-literal model ids and is the speculative-complexity T6 warns against.

confirmed.solution: Make the hook events generic over the agent's TContext/TOutput so payloads are typed, e.g. `public data class OnFinishEvent<TContext, TOutput>(val finalOutput: TOutput, ..., val experimentalContext: TContext?)`. Where the hook is wired through a non-reified boundary, prefer a typed holder over Any?. If full genericization is too invasive before freeze, at minimum wrap in a `public class TypedOpaque<T>(val value: T)`-style carrier so the cast is centralized and the surface can evolve. The key point: decide and freeze the typed shape NOW — Any? is the one shape you can never widen later.
confirmed.builderTaskHint: Genericize OnStartEvent/OnFinishEvent (and StepResult.experimentalContext) over TContext/TOutput so the Any? fields become typed. This rewrites their klib/JVM signatures — must precede 1.0 freeze. If genericization ripples too far, escalate to the agent-loop owner; do not silently leave Any? frozen. Regenerate both api dumps and update LifecycleHooksTest.
confirmed.dimension: KMP library API hygiene & binary compatibility

verdict.critique:
VERIFIED REAL. All four cited fields exist exactly as quoted in src/commonMain/kotlin/ai/torad/aisdk/Lifecycle.kt: OnStartEvent.options: Any? (line 17), OnFinishEvent.finalOutput: Any? (line 57), OnFinishEvent.experimental_context: Any? (line 77, with the self-incriminating doc comment "consumers cast to their TContext"), and StepResult.experimentalContext: Any? (line 152). The erasure is not inherent — the surrounding agent retains the types. ToolLoopAgent is declared ToolLoopAgent<TContext, TOutput> (line 60) and constructs these events from already-typed values: OnStartEvent(prompt, priorMessages, validatedOptions) where validatedOptions is TContext? (line 496), OnFinishEvent(finalOutput=..., experimental_context=activeContext) where activeContext is TContext? and finalOutput comes from decodeFinalOutput(...): TOutput (lines 446, 1107-1115), and StepResult(experimentalContext = activeContext) (line 1051). So the type is present at every construction site and deliberately widened to Any? at the public boundary. The finding is accurate and the bad-pattern characterization (unchecked casts pushed onto every host) is fair.

SOLUTION CORRECT, with a caveat on scope. Genericizing OnStartEvent<TContext> and OnFinishEvent<TContext, TOutput> is feasible and idiomatic precisely because the agent is already generic; onStart/onFinish hook signatures (suspend OnStartEvent.() -> Unit, etc.) trivially become OnStartEvent<TContext>.() -> Unit since they're declared inside the generic class. The solution is NOT a half-measure: its primary recommendation removes Any? entirely; the TypedOpaque<T> fallback is explicitly a fallback, and even that eliminates the raw Any? (centralizes the cast in one typed carrier). The one real complication, correctly flagged in builderTaskHint, is StepResult: it appears in PrepareStepScope.steps, OnStepStartEvent.priorSteps, OnStepFinishEvent.step, and OnAbortEvent.steps, so genericizing StepResult<TContext> ripples across the whole hook surface. That ripple is acceptable (the agent already threads TContext through all of these) but is more than effort "M" if StepResult is included.

TENET ALIGNED. "typed models over Map<String,JsonElement>/Any?" is an explicit tenet; this is a textbook instance. No sealed-interface/Flow/nullable-pair concerns introduced.

ABI ASSESSMENT CORRECT. breaksAbi=true is right for klib targets (ios/linux/wasm): type parameters are part of the .klib ABI, and adding them rewrites the signatures recorded in api/torad-aisdk.klib.api. On JVM the erased field type stays Object, but the binary-compatibility-validator .api dump still changes (class becomes generic) and metadata changes force recompilation. Crucially, the timing framing is right: the library is at 0.3.0-alpha01, NOT yet frozen at 1.0, so this is exactly the pre-freeze window to make the break additively-free. The finding's "frozen at 1.0 / must precede freeze" urgency is well-timed, not alarmist.
verdict.refinedSolution:
Do the full genericization NOW (pre-1.0-alpha is the correct, free window). Concretely:

1. OnStartEvent<TContext>(prompt, priorMessages, options: TContext?). Drop Any?.
2. OnFinishEvent<TContext, TOutput>(finalOutput: TOutput, totalSteps, usage, pendingApprovals, messages, experimentalContext: TContext?). Also rename experimental_context -> experimentalContext to match Kotlin convention and the rest of the codebase (StepResult already uses experimentalContext); the snake_case here is TS residue.
3. StepResult<TContext> with experimentalContext: TContext?. Accept the ripple — thread TContext through OnStepStartEvent<TContext>(priorSteps: List<StepResult<TContext>>), OnStepFinishEvent<TContext>(step: StepResult<TContext>), OnAbortEvent<TContext>(steps), and PrepareStepScope.steps. The agent already carries TContext at all these sites, so this is mechanical, not invasive. This raises true effort to M/L, not M — set expectations accordingly.
4. Hook field types in ToolLoopAgent update for free since they're nested in the generic class (onStart: suspend OnStartEvent<TContext>.() -> Unit, etc.).
5. Do NOT ship the TypedOpaque<T> fallback. It is strictly worse than full genericization here because the agent already has the type parameters in scope — a wrapper would re-introduce an unwrap step for no compatibility benefit. Reserve that pattern only for a genuinely non-reified boundary, which this is not.
6. Regenerate BOTH api/jvm/torad-aisdk.api and api/torad-aisdk.klib.api, and update LifecycleHooksTest to assert the typed payloads (e.g. a TContext data class round-trips without a cast).

Rejected alternative: leaving finalOutput: Any? "because Output<TOutput> may be null and text falls back to String". decodeFinalOutput already returns TOutput (with the (text as TOutput) bridge for the no-Output String case), so finalOutput: TOutput is sound — the existing unchecked cast lives in the agent, not the public surface, exactly where it belongs.

@Deprecated("Use streamText(output = ...) instead.")

confirmed.badPattern: All 4 @Deprecated annotations in the public surface are message-only: no `ReplaceWith(...)` (so no automated IDE migration) and no explicit `level = DeprecationLevel` (so they stay WARNING indefinitely with no defined path to ERROR/HIDDEN). Give every @Deprecated a ReplaceWith expression and an explicit level, e.g.:
@Deprecated(

)

confirmed.title: Hosted/provider-executed tools are typed Tool<JsonElement, JsonElement, Any?> — raw-JSON I/O and Any? context baked into the public type
confirmed.tenet: T2 (separate wire from domain) / typed models over JsonElement+Any?

evidence.snippet:
val parallelSearch: Tool<JsonElement, JsonElement, Any?> = providerExecutedTool(
    name = "parallelSearch", ... inputSerializer = JsonElement.serializer(), outputSerializer = JsonElement.serializer())
public fun codeExecution(args: JsonElement = JsonObject(emptyMap())): Tool<JsonElement, JsonElement, Any?> =
public fun <TInput, TOutput, TContext> providerExecutedTool(  // the factory; hosted tools pin all three type params to JsonElement/JsonElement/Any?

confirmed.badPattern: Every hosted provider tool (web search, code execution, file search, etc. — 30 public declarations) is exposed as Tool<JsonElement, JsonElement, Any?>: untyped wire JSON for both input and output and Any? for context. Callers get no schema, no typed result, and Any? context that defeats the Tool's TContext generic. These types are frozen in api/*.api. While these tools execute provider-side (so input/output are genuinely opaque-ish), the Any? context in particular is gratuitous — it could be the caller's TContext.
confirmed.solution: Two fixes: (1) Parameterize the context: `public fun <TContext> webSearch(...): Tool<JsonElement, JsonElement, TContext>` so a hosted tool slots into a typed ToolSet<TContext> without erasing context to Any?. (2) For the JsonElement I/O, introduce thin typed wire models per hosted tool where the provider shape is known (e.g. WebSearchResult) and keep JsonElement only where the provider truly returns opaque JSON — document which. At minimum, do the context fix; it is the part that needlessly leaks Any? into a generic-aware API.
confirmed.builderTaskHint: Parameterize hosted-tool factories (Gateway.kt:370/376, XaiProvider.kt:112-130, AzureProvider.kt:50+, providerExecutedTool at Tool.kt:698) over TContext instead of pinning Any?. Changes klib/JVM generic signatures — land before 1.0. NOTE: this is the lowest-severity finding I kept; I intentionally dropped two lower-value items to stay within the cap: (a) StepResult.providerMetadata / many event fields use Map<String, JsonElement> (raw wire bag) — same class of leak as ABI-04/ABI-06, fold into those fixes; (b) the engine state type ToolLoopAgentState is flag-shaped (isStreaming/isModelLoading/error?) rather than a sealed phase — real but owned by the agent-loop dimension, not pure API hygiene. Flag both to the orchestrator if a follow-up pass has budget.
confirmed.dimension: KMP library API hygiene & binary compatibility

verdict.refinedSolution: Prefer declaration-site contravariance on Tool: add the in modifier to the third type parameter (Tool of TInput, TOutput, in TContext). TContext appears only in consuming positions (the ToolExecutionContext receiver of execute, and ToolPredicateOptions), so in is legal. Then a Tool with Any? context becomes a subtype of a Tool with the caller AppContext and every existing hosted tool, including the eager XaiTools/GatewayTools holders and the xaiTools/gatewayTools singletons, composes into a typed tool set with zero changes to the 30 sites. Verify it compiles across StreamingTool, LambdaTool, LambdaStreamingTool subclasses (Tool.kt 200, 251, 295). ABI change to the generic signature; land before 1.0. As a follow-on ergonomic nicety, also make the factories generic over context (webSearch returning Tool of JsonElement, JsonElement, TContext) so a hosted tool can be minted at the caller context type, but note this alone does not fix the data-class holders or val singletons whose fields stay Any? unless variance lands. For wire types keep input JsonElement (opaque provider args); introduce typed output models only where the provider result shape is stable and public (e.g. WebSearchResult) and document which tools stay JsonElement because the payload is genuinely opaque. Do not fabricate typed shapes for unstable payloads.
verdict.critique: Evidence verified by opening every cited file. Gateway.kt 369-381 declares parallelSearch/perplexitySearch as Tool of JsonElement,JsonElement,Any? via providerExecutedTool; XaiProvider.kt 100-131 declares 7 hosted tools plus the codeExecution factory at 112 returning the same type; the private xaiProviderTool helper (385-399) also pins Any?; Tool.kt 698 is the generic providerExecutedTool factory callers collapse to JsonElement/JsonElement/Any?. The pattern recurs in AnthropicProvider.kt 1154, OpenAIProvider.kt 191, GoogleProvider.kt 2031, OpenAICompatibleProviderFacades.kt 269, so ~30 hosted-tool declarations is plausible, not inflated. All sit in api/*.api (explicitApi ON), frozen. CRUX the finding gets right: TContext on Tool (Tool.kt 70) has no variance annotation, so it is invariant; ToolSet stores Map of Tool with star projections over the same TContext (463-464). A Tool with Any? context only unifies with a ToolSet of Any?, so an app with a typed AppContext tool set cannot add a hosted tool without erasing the whole set to Any?. The Any? is not merely honest; it actively blocks composition into a typed tool set. Medium severity is fair. The context fix (generic factories) is idiomatic and compiles: providerExecutedTool is already generic over context and the body never reads context (throws if executed locally, 711-718), sound for any context; not a half-measure since it fully removes Any?. Wire-model prong is properly hedged. breaksAbi true is correct: a new type parameter changes the JVM/klib generic signature; land before 1.0 (0.3.0-alpha01). tenetAligned: typed-over-Any?/JsonElement, no sealed/Flow regression. Two caveats the solution understates: (1) the convenience holders (data class XaiTools at 100, eager val xaiTools at 110, GatewayTools) cannot be generic so their fields stay Any?; generic factories only help callers who call the factory directly, leaving the singletons pinned, which the finding does not acknowledge. (2) declaration-site contravariance (in TContext) is the more surgical alternative that fixes all 30 sites and the singletons in one edit; the finding misses it.

confirmed.title: Stream protocol violations are reported as freeform String StreamEvent.Error with the typed cause slot left null, while the non-stream path throws typed WireDecodeException
confirmed.tenet: T1/T2 (typed protocol states; a typed error model exists but is not used at the stream boundary)

evidence.snippet:
private fun missingIdentityError(eventType: String, field: String): StreamEvent.Error =
    StreamEvent.Error("Open Responses stream protocol error: $eventType missing required $field.")  // no cause = WireDecodeException(...)
public data class Error(val message: String, @Transient val cause: Throwable? = null) : StreamEvent  // typed cause slot exists, never populated by parsers
?: return listOf(StreamEvent.Error("Hugging Face stream protocol error: response.output_item.added missing item."))

confirmed.badPattern: return listOf(StreamEvent.Error("...protocol error..."))  // String only, cause = null, no WireDecodeException
confirmed.solution:
The non-stream paths throw WireDecodeException (carrying provider/operation/path/value). The stream paths emit a String and drop all of that structure even though StreamEvent.Error already has a `cause: Throwable?` slot. Populate it so consumers can pattern-match the typed failure. BEFORE:
  StreamEvent.Error("Open Responses stream protocol error: $eventType missing required $field.")
AFTER:
  val e = WireDecodeException("Open Responses", "stream event", field, "missing required $field in $eventType")
  StreamEvent.Error(e.message ?: "protocol error", cause = e)
This unifies the two error channels on one typed exception type without changing the public StreamEvent shape.
confirmed.builderTaskHint: Touch OpenResponsesProvider.missingIdentityError (:1036), HuggingFaceProvider stream errors (:521,:545), HttpTransport.kt:339, and any provider stream-state returning StreamEvent.Error(String). Lower priority than WIRE-01/02/03; do after the identity-fabrication fixes since those add new StreamEvent.Error call-sites that should use the typed form from the start.
confirmed.astGrepRule:
id: stringly-stream-error
language: kotlin
rule:
  pattern: StreamEvent.Error($MSG)
  not:
    has:
      pattern: cause = $C
confirmed.dimension: Provider/wire boundary & typed protocol states

verdict.critique:
Every cited file:line confirmed by reading the source. OpenResponsesProvider.kt:1036-1037 returns StreamEvent.Error(String) with no cause; HuggingFaceProvider.kt:521 and :545 do the same; HttpTransport.kt:339 discards result.error (an available Throwable) into a String. Streaming.kt:301-305 shows StreamEvent.Error has a @Transient cause: Throwable? slot whose own doc comment says it is meant to carry the typed cause "to the boundary" — and the parsers never use it. WireDecodeException (WireDecoder.kt:18, constructor provider/operation/path/message/value/cause) is the typed exception the non-stream path throws via fail() at :146, so the asymmetry between the two error channels is real.

The finding is strengthened by evidence the reviewer did not cite: of 45 StreamEvent.Error( call-sites, exactly 3 populate cause (ToolLoopAgent.kt:511/598/797), all forwarding genuinely-caught Throwables (prepareCall/prepareStep/model failed). So the "emit Error with typed cause" convention ALREADY exists in the agent loop; the wire parsers simply diverge from it. The fix brings parsers into line with an established in-repo pattern rather than inventing one.

solutionCorrect: the proposed AFTER compiles against the real constructor (positional provider, operation, path=field, message), e.message is non-null so the ?: fallback is harmless, and crucially it is NOT a half-measure — a Flow stage returning List<StreamEvent> cannot throw the way the non-stream path does, so carrying WireDecodeException in the cause slot is the correct unification of the two channels (replacing the stringly form, not adding beside it). tenetAligned: T2 (typed cause crosses boundary), T4 (emit, not throw, respecting the Flow contract), reuses existing typed exception, no sentinel/nullable-pair issues. abiAssessment: breaksAbi=false is correct — StreamEvent.Error shape is unchanged (cause already has a default), edits are at internal parser call-sites only; api/*.api unaffected.

Severity medium is fair. Note the surrounding lines (itemId .orEmpty() at :522/546, ?: settings.generateId() at :553) are separate identity-fabrication findings (WIRE-01/02), correctly NOT conflated here.

confirmed.title: Providers decode raw JsonObject directly into domain types with ad-hoc key lookups; no @Serializable wire DTO layer (root enabler of WIRE-01..04). Report claim #12 is now STALE.
confirmed.tenet: T2 (separate transport/wire types from domain types — raw provider JSON must not leak into domain construction), T1, T7

evidence.snippet: obj["functionCall"]?.jsonObject?.let { callObj -> ContentPart.ToolCall(toolCallId = callObj["id"]?... , toolName = callObj["name"]?... , input = callObj["args"] ?: JsonObject(emptyMap())) }  // raw JsonObject → domain, hand-rolled keys
val itemId = item["id"]?.jsonPrimitive?.contentOrNull ?: obj["item_id"]?.jsonPrimitive?.contentOrNull.orEmpty()  // wire-shape knowledge smeared across the parser

Evidence: OpenResponsesProvider.kt:936, :953, :954 ... AnthropicProvider.kt:843  — these lines are ALREADY FIXED (now emit missingIdentityError / WireDecoder.fail); the live instances are HuggingFace/Google/Bedrock/OpenAICompatible/Cohere, which the report does not name.

confirmed.badPattern: Constructing domain ContentPart/EmbeddingModelResult directly from raw JsonObject key lookups (wire["x"]?.jsonPrimitive?.contentOrNull) instead of decoding a @Serializable wire DTO at the boundary
confirmed.solution:
Per-provider parsing repeats wire-schema knowledge inline, which is exactly what lets sentinels (WIRE-01..03) creep in unnoticed. The structural fix is a thin @Serializable wire layer decoded via WireDecoder.decode, then mapped to domain. BEFORE: scattered obj["id"]/obj["name"]/obj["call_id"] reads. AFTER (sketch):
  @Serializable private data class GoogleFunctionCall(val id: String? = null, val name: String, val args: JsonElement = JsonObject(emptyMap()))
  val fc = WireDecoder.decode(GoogleFunctionCall.serializer(), callObj, "google", "response content", path)
  ContentPart.ToolCall(toolCallId = fc.id ?: WireDecoder.fail(...), toolName = fc.name, input = fc.args)
This makes `name` non-null at the type level (decode fails if absent) and confines wire-shape knowledge to one DTO per provider. Large; recommend doing it provider-by-provider, starting with the ones touched in WIRE-01.
confirmed.builderTaskHint: This is the durable root-cause fix behind WIRE-01..04 (do those tactical fixes first; this is the strategic follow-up). VERIFY/REFUTE outcome: I CONFIRM the report's claim #12 *class* (stream parsers fabricate tool identities) but REFUTE its evidence — the cited OpenResponses :936/:953/:954 and Anthropic :843 are already fixed in the working tree; the real remaining sites are in HuggingFace/Google/Bedrock/OpenAICompatible/Cohere (see WIRE-01/02). DROPPED lower-severity items intentionally omitted to keep the top-8 focused: (a) transcript-text .orEmpty() in Gladia/Deepgram/ElevenLabs/Revai/Hume (text, not correlation keys); (b) Google MCP toolName fallback to literal "mcp_server_tool" / type.removeSuffix("_call") at :989/:991/:1000 (fold into WIRE-01); (c) hosted provider tools typed as Tool<JsonElement, JsonElement, Any?> (Gateway.kt:370, XaiProvider/GoogleProvider hosted-tool factories) — confirms report claim #6 but is a separate public-API typing dimension, not wire-decoding.
confirmed.dimension: Provider/wire boundary & typed protocol states

verdict.refinedSolution: Add one thin private Serializable wire DTO per provider response shape, decode via the existing WireDecoder.decode, then map to domain. Make required fields non-null at the type level (e.g. name as non-null String so decode fails when absent) so orEmpty and id-generator sentinels become impossible. Do tactical WIRE-01 through 04 fixes first, then migrate provider-by-provider starting with Google and HuggingFace; the non-stream generateGoogleResult is the laggard since streaming paths already use WireDecoder. Decide the id contract per provider: where a tool-call id is guaranteed, make it required so decode fails loudly; keep an id fallback only where absence is documented. Keep DTOs private to preserve ABI. Treat transcript-text orEmpty and hosted-tool generic typing as separate items.
verdict.critique:
Evidence verified by reading the files. GoogleProvider.kt:1530 constructs ContentPart.ToolCall directly from raw callObj key lookups with two sentinels: toolName uses orEmpty (T5 empty-string fabrication for a required correlation field) and toolCallId uses an id-generator fallback. HuggingFaceProvider.kt:522 confirms the same class: itemId falls back to orEmpty. No Serializable wire DTO layer exists; wire-schema knowledge is smeared inline across non-stream parse paths (T2). The finding's self-assessment is accurate: OpenResponsesProvider function_call now returns missingIdentityError and AnthropicProvider uses WireDecoder.objectValue, so report claim 12's cited OpenResponses 936/953/954 and Anthropic 843 are already remediated; live laggards are Google/HuggingFace/Bedrock/OpenAICompatible/Cohere. Breadth is real (77/50/47 raw-lookup hits in top three providers). The solution is compilable and genuinely better: WireDecoder.decode with the serializer/value/provider/operation/path signature exists, json is shared, so decoding a private DTO then mapping to domain is idiomatic. It is NOT a half-measure. ABI is correct: DTOs are private, domain ContentPart untouched, breaksAbi=false.</critique>
</invoke>

confirmed.title: 7 private provider stream-state machines (`accept(): List<StreamEvent>`) are pure deterministic parsers but untestable in isolation; 6 of 7 have ~zero protocol-error/identity-fallback coverage
confirmed.tenet: T7 (hard-to-test private unit not yet extracted) + risky path lacking tests

evidence.snippet: fun accept(chunk: JsonElement): List<StreamEvent> {  // pure JSON->events state machine, but enclosing class is `private class AnthropicStreamState` (:834)
val id = if (isToolBlock) { block["id"]?.jsonPrimitive?.contentOrNull?.takeIf { it.isNotBlank() } ?: return listOf(missingToolIdentityError(type, "id")) }  // riskiest branch, private
private class OpenResponsesStreamState(  // accept() at :931, missingIdentityError at :1036 — OpenResponsesProviderTest has 0 protocol-error assertions
private class BedrockStreamState(  // accept(value: JsonObject): List<StreamEvent> at :1054 — AmazonBedrockProviderTest has 0 protocol-error assertions

confirmed.badPattern: A provider's entire wire-protocol decoder is a `private class XStreamState { fun accept(JsonElement): List<StreamEvent> }`. The method is already pure and synchronous (the ideal unit), but `private` forces every test to drive it through `ToadLoopAgent`/Ktor + the in-test `TestServer` SSE harness. The friction means branch coverage is written only where someone bothered: AnthropicProviderTest (modified Jun 20) covers malformed delta / missing id / missing name / unknown index / malformed tool JSON, while OpenResponses, Bedrock, and HuggingFace stream parsers have 0 protocol-error assertions despite identical risky branches.
confirmed.solution: Change the 7 `private class *StreamState` to `internal class` (they hold no public types — `internal` does not appear in the public ABI). Then add table-driven `commonTest` unit tests that feed crafted `JsonElement` chunks straight into `accept(...)`/`finish()` and assert the returned `List<StreamEvent>`, one row per protocol branch (happy path, missing identity, malformed JSON, unknown index, fallback id). Reuse the Anthropic error-branch test matrix for the other 6 providers. BEFORE: `private class OpenResponsesStreamState(...)`; AFTER: `internal class OpenResponsesStreamState(...)` + `@Test fun functionCallMissingCallIdEmitsError() { val s = OpenResponsesStreamState(aiSdkJson); val e = s.accept(json.parseToJsonElement("""{\"type\":\"response.output_item.done\",\"item\":{\"type\":\"function_call\",\"id\":\"i1\",\"name\":\"x\"}}""")); assertTrue((e.single() as StreamEvent.Error).message.contains("missing required call_id")) }`
confirmed.builderTaskHint: Flip the 7 `private class *StreamState` to `internal` (AnthropicProvider:834, AmazonBedrockProvider:1042, GoogleProvider:1042 & :1573, HuggingFaceProvider:495, OpenResponsesProvider:921, OpenAICompatibleProvider:856). Add one `*StreamStateTest` per provider with a table of crafted JSON chunks hitting every error/fallback branch in `accept()`/`finish()`. Verify `api/*.api` diff is empty (internal must not surface). Run all provider tests.
confirmed.dimension: Testability & composability gaps

verdict.critique:
Structural evidence verified at every cited line. All 7 `private class *StreamState` exist (Anthropic:834, OpenResponses:921, Bedrock:1042, Google:1042/:1573, HuggingFace:495, OpenAICompatible:856). The `accept(JsonElement|JsonObject): List<StreamEvent>` signatures and the Anthropic:886-888 tool-identity-fallback branch are exactly as cited. The classes are synchronous decoders driven only through Ktor/TestServer SSE in tests, so the testability/composability concern (T7: a hard-to-test private unit not yet extracted) is genuine.

The proposed fix is sound and idiomatic for this repo: flipping `private`→`internal` exposes the units to commonTest via the associated-compilation friend path (already used — SchemaSanitizer:8 and MistralResponseTransform:9 are `internal`), and with `explicitApi()` ON (build.gradle.kts:32) `internal` never enters the public ABI, so breaksAbi=false is correct. Constructor examples in the solution match reality (`OpenResponsesStreamState(json: Json)`; `aiSdkJson` is a real internal instance). No tenet is violated by the change; it is not a half-measure (it changes the actual visibility, not bolt-on duplication).

Two factual overstatements drag the finding's framing: (1) "OpenResponses, Bedrock, and HuggingFace stream parsers have 0 protocol-error assertions" is FALSE for two of three — OpenResponsesProviderTest:250-317 has 4+ stream `StreamEvent.Error` protocol assertions (missing item_id / name / call_id / output item) and HuggingFaceProviderTest:314-328 asserts the stream missing-item error. Only Bedrock (its 2 error matches are SigV4 clock-skew, not stream-parse branches), OpenAICompatible (0) and Google (1) are genuinely thin. So "6 of 7 have ~zero coverage" should be "3 of 7 are genuinely thin." (2) "pure deterministic parsers" is loose — `accept()` mutates instance state (blocks map, finishReason), so it is a stateful state machine; tests for some branches must drive an ordered prefix, not a single isolated chunk. These inflate severity but do not falsify the core anti-pattern, which is real and confirmed by reading the files.
verdict.refinedSolution:
Flip the 7 `private class *StreamState` to `internal` (Anthropic:834, OpenResponses:921, Bedrock:1042, Google:1042 & :1573, HuggingFace:495, OpenAICompatible:856). With explicitApi() ON, internal stays out of api/*.api — verify the ABI diff is empty after the change. Add one table-driven `*StreamStateTest` per provider in commonTest, constructing the state directly (e.g. `OpenResponsesStreamState(aiSdkJson)`) and feeding crafted `JsonElement` chunks into `accept(...)`/`finish()`.

Correct the coverage targeting: prioritize the three genuinely under-tested parsers — AmazonBedrock, OpenAICompatible, and Google — which have ~zero stream-protocol-error assertions. OpenResponses and HuggingFace already assert stream `StreamEvent.Error` branches via the TestServer harness; for those, internalization mainly removes harness friction and lets the existing matrix be expressed as cheap unit rows.

Because `accept()` is stateful (mutates blocks/finishReason/toolCalls), write rows as ordered chunk sequences where the branch requires it (e.g. Anthropic `message_start` → `content_block_start` with a tool block missing `id`), not isolated single chunks, then assert the returned `List<StreamEvent>` (happy path, missing identity → error, malformed JSON → error, unknown index, id fallback). Reuse the Anthropic error matrix as the template. No sealed-type or Flow-contract changes are needed; the providers' public `stream(): Flow<StreamEvent>` boundary is untouched.

confirmed.title: `ToolLoopAgent.streamInternal` is a ~660-line private flow{} god-method that inlines five distinct phases, so inner decisions are only reachable end-to-end
confirmed.tenet: T7 (a hard-to-test unit not yet extracted) + T8 (god file/method)

evidence.snippet: private fun streamInternal(  // body runs from 467 to ~1130 (next private fun applyToolApprovalResponses is at 1130)
resolveCall(toolDef, call, messages.toList())  // tool-call resolve+repair categorization inlined into the flow body
coroutineScope { val signals = Channel<ParallelToolSignal>(Channel.BUFFERED) ... }  // entire bounded parallel-execution engine inlined (937-1028)
val step = StepResult( stepNumber = stepNumber, text = stepText.toString(), ... )  // step assembly inlined at the tail of the same flow{}

confirmed.badPattern: One `private fun streamInternal(...): Flow<StreamEvent>` whose single `flow{}` body sequentially performs: (1) per-step model-stream consumption, (2) tool-call resolve+repair categorization (820-924), (3) approval gating, (4) bounded parallel tool execution via Channel+Semaphore (937-1028), and (5) StepResult assembly (1036+). Each phase has real branching (repair retry, approval-vs-execute split, ordered application) but none is independently invokable — every assertion about phase 2/4/5 must boot a full fake model + drain the whole Flow. The many extracted helpers (executeTool, applyToolResult, callNeedsApproval) are good, but the orchestration that wires them is itself the untested 660-line unit.
confirmed.solution: Extract the inlined phases into `internal` suspend functions / Flow stages that the orchestrator composes, mirroring how `executeTool`/`applyToolResult` were already pulled out. E.g. `internal fun categorizeToolCalls(calls, stepTools, messages): ToolCallPlan` (returns the gated/executable/skipped split as a typed value per T3), and `internal fun runParallelTools(plan, ...): Flow<ParallelToolSignal>` returning the ordered-completion stream so the engine just `emitAll`s it. Each becomes directly unit-testable. BEFORE: a 660-line `flow{}`; AFTER: `flow{ ...; val plan = categorizeToolCalls(...); emitAll(runParallelTools(plan, ...)); emit(assembleStep(...)) }`. Keep the public `stream()`/`generate()` signatures identical.
confirmed.builderTaskHint: This is a structural extraction, not a behavior change — guard it with the existing end-to-end suite (ParallelToolExecutionTest, ToolApprovalTest, ToolCallRepairTest, ToolLoopAgent*Test) staying green. Extract categorizeToolCalls (logic at 820-924) and runParallelTools (937-1028) as internal members returning typed values, then add focused unit tests for the categorization split and ordered-application logic. Confirm `api/*.api` unchanged.
confirmed.dimension: Testability & composability gaps

verdict.refinedSolution: Extract two internal members and make categorization pure (T3). Add a typed plan: internal data class ResolvedExecutable holding call, tool, input; and internal sealed class ToolCallPlan with base val call and variants Execute, NeedsApproval, Failed carrying AgentError, ProviderExecuted. This replaces the ad-hoc map of toolCallId to Pair plus parallel lists. internal suspend fun categorizeToolCalls(calls, stepTools, activeContext, messages) returning List of ToolCallPlan moves logic from 819-890 WITHOUT touching the FlowCollector: NoSuchTool, decode/repair failure, and gated-after-repair all become Failed; the orchestrator emits errors for Failed and signs/emits approval requests for NeedsApproval. internal fun runParallelTools returning Flow of OrderedToolCompletion wraps 937-1028 (channelFlow or coroutineScope owning Semaphore and launch fan-out) and emits ordered completions; convert OrderedToolCompletion from private sealed interface to internal sealed CLASS with index in the base constructor (T1). Orchestrator collects it and applies results in original call order into messages and stepToolResults at the single collector site. Optionally extract assembleStep from 1036. ABI: all new members internal; run gradle apiCheck to confirm api jvm and klib files unchanged. Guard with existing ParallelToolExecutionTest, ToolApprovalTest, ToolCallRepairTest, ModelRepromptRepairTest, ToolApprovalDenialTest green, then add focused unit tests for the categorization split and the ordered-application invariant under maxParallelToolCalls above 1.
verdict.critique: VERIFIED REAL. streamInternal at line 467 returns a Flow via one flow block whose body runs to about 1129 (next member applyToolApprovalResponses is line 1130), roughly 660 lines. Phases confirmed by reading the file: resolve/repair/approval categorization inlined 819-890 (resolveCall at 830, callNeedsApproval at 849); approval gating and signing 896-921; bounded parallel engine inlined 937-1028 (coroutineScope, Channel BUFFERED, Semaphore, launch, ordered fan-in apply); StepResult assembly at 1036+. Supporting types ParallelToolSignal (1791), OrderedToolCompletion (1797, a private sealed interface), ChannelToolEventCollector (1809) and helpers executeTool/applyToolResult/callNeedsApproval all exist, confirming helpers are decomposed but the orchestration wiring is the untested unit. Cited test files exist. Solution is genuinely better (T7/T8), removes the inlined phases, all extractions internal so breaksAbi false is correct for this explicitApi library. Caveats folded into refinedSolution: categorization emits errors mid-loop (823, 838-846, 853-860, 869-882) so a T3-clean version must return errors as typed plan data rather than take the FlowCollector; the new plan type should be a sealed class not interface (T1); ordered application into mutable messages must stay in the orchestrator collector. Severity high is slightly generous (medium-high) but the core claim is accurate.

confirmed.title: `OpenResponsesStreamState` fabricates empty-string stream-part ids via `.orEmpty()` on reasoning/message/text paths — an untested sentinel branch that silently mismatches start/delta/end ids
confirmed.tenet: T5 (sentinel fabrication: id meaning both present and "") surfaced by a missing test

evidence.snippet: val itemId = item["id"]?.jsonPrimitive?.contentOrNull ?: obj["item_id"]?.jsonPrimitive?.contentOrNull.orEmpty()  // reasoning start -> ReasoningStart("")
id = obj["item_id"]?.jsonPrimitive?.contentOrNull.orEmpty(),  // response.output_text.delta -> TextDelta(id="")
?: return listOf(missingIdentityError(type, "name"))  // the function_call path correctly errors on missing identity — the text/reasoning paths do NOT, they fall back to ""

confirmed.badPattern: Within the same `accept()` state machine, tool-call identity is treated as load-bearing (missing -> `missingIdentityError`), but text/reasoning identity is silently coerced to `""` via `?: ...orEmpty()`. If the wire omits `item_id` (or the `item.id`), `ReasoningStart("")`/`TextStart("")` is emitted and a later `TextDelta(item_id="abc")` no longer matches it, so streamed text is dropped — and because the parser is `private` with 0 protocol-error tests for OpenResponses, nothing catches it.
confirmed.solution: Make the text/reasoning paths use the same identity discipline as the function_call path: resolve via `itemIdFromItem(item, obj)` / `itemIdFromEvent(obj)` and `?: return listOf(missingIdentityError(type, "item_id"))` instead of `.orEmpty()`. Then (after TEST-01 makes the class `internal`) add a unit test asserting that a missing-id reasoning/text event yields a `StreamEvent.Error`, and that a present id round-trips start->delta->end. BEFORE: `?: obj["item_id"]...orEmpty()`; AFTER: `?: itemIdFromEvent(obj) ?: return listOf(missingIdentityError(type, "item_id"))`.
confirmed.builderTaskHint: Fix the 6 `.orEmpty()` id fallbacks in OpenResponsesStreamState.accept (lines 950, 955, 989, 994, 1003, 1007) to use the identity-or-error pattern already used for function_call (978-983). Audit the other 6 StreamState parsers for the same `.orEmpty()`-on-id smell while you are there. Add the round-trip + missing-id unit tests. No public-signature change.
confirmed.dimension: Testability & composability gaps

verdict.refinedSolution: Use the same identity-or-error pattern as function_call. For lines 950, 955, 989, 994 replace the orEmpty fallback with: itemIdFromItem(item, obj) else return listOf(missingIdentityError(type, item_id)). For line 1007 use: itemIdFromEvent(obj) else return listOf(missingIdentityError(type, item_id)). For line 1003 remove ONLY the reasoning-0 literal but KEEP activeReasoningId, i.e. itemIdFromEvent(obj) elvis activeReasoningId elvis return missingIdentityError, where each elvis is the Kotlin elvis operator. Wrap the single-expression delta branches at 1001 and 1006 in braces so the early return, which returns from accept whose return type is a list of StreamEvent, is well-formed and returns before appending the malformed event. Add tests in OpenResponsesProviderTest.kt; no visibility change is needed since they flow through the public stream like the existing tests at 250, 275, 302. Tests: a missing-id reasoning event, a missing-id message event, and a missing-id output_text.delta each yield a single StreamEvent.Error whose message contains item_id; a reasoning delta with no item_id falls back to the active reasoning id, asserting ReasoningDelta id equals rs_1; and a present id round-trips start to delta to end with id equality. No public signature change, breaksAbi false. Also audit the other StreamState parsers, namely Anthropic, Google, Cohere, Bedrock, HuggingFace, for the same orEmpty-on-id smell, but convert only id sites required for grouping, not non-id delta-text fields, since 1004 and 1008 are correctly defaulted.
verdict.critique: Evidence verified by reading OpenResponsesProvider.kt. Lines 950, 955, 989, 994, 1007 fabricate empty-string ids via orEmpty on reasoning/message start, reasoning/message end, and output_text.delta; line 1003 fabricates a reasoning-0 literal sentinel. All 6 are id-sentinel fabrications (5 orEmpty plus 1 literal), so the count is directionally right though imprecisely described. The asymmetry is real and in the same accept body: function_call paths at 942, 961, 966, 976 resolve identity via itemIdFromItem and itemIdFromEvent and hard-error via missingIdentityError, while text and reasoning silently coerce to empty. Helpers itemIdFromItem at 1029, itemIdFromEvent at 1033, missingIdentityError at 1036 already exist with takeIf isNotBlank. Downstream id is load-bearing: StreamEvent text ids are non-null String in Streaming.kt 78-95 and ToUIMessageStream.kt 37-42 emits text-start, text-delta, text-end keyed by id, so a TextStart with empty id followed by TextDelta with msg_1 fails to group, confirming the text-drop failure mode. Test gap confirmed: OpenResponsesProviderTest.kt has missing-id protocol-error tests for function_call at 250 and 275 but none for text or reasoning. ABI: OpenResponsesStreamState is private, no public-signature change, breaksAbi false is correct, since the degraded-empty-id to StreamEvent.Error shift is a semantic change not an ABI change. T5 aligned. One solution defect: it lumps line 1003 in and would drop the legitimate activeReasoningId stateful fallback that is intentional per comment 999-1000, where real OpenAI sends reasoning_summary_text.delta carrying the id via activeReasoningId from reasoning-start; only the reasoning-0 literal should be removed and activeReasoningId preserved.

confirmed.title: No test exercises `executeTool`'s CancellationException-rethrow for a cancelled tool body — the path explicitly guarding against persisting cancellation as a tool result
confirmed.tenet: risky path (cancellation) lacking a test; T7
confirmed.badPattern: The agent has a security/correctness-critical invariant (a tool body that is cancelled must abort the loop, not be recorded as `ToolExecutionResult.Failure` and continue the conversation). Cancellation is tested at the transport level (CompletionApiTest), hook level (TelemetryWiringTest), and chat level (ChatConcurrencyTest), but no test makes a *tool's execute()* throw `CancellationException` inside a running agent step and asserts the exception propagates out of `generate()`/`stream()` while no tool-failure ToolResult is appended. This is exactly the branch a future refactor could silently break by widening the lower `catch (t: Throwable)`.
confirmed.solution: Add a `commonTest` case using the existing `TestToolLoopAgent` + a one-tool model: the tool's body `throw CancellationException("x")`. Assert `assertFailsWith<CancellationException> { agent.generate(...) }` and that the collected stream contains no `StreamEvent.ToolResult`/`ToolError` for that call (i.e. cancellation was not converted to a Failure). Add a sibling case for the parallel path (2 tools, one throws CE) asserting the whole `coroutineScope` tears down.
confirmed.builderTaskHint: Add ToolCancellationTest with two cases (single tool throws CE; one of two parallel tools throws CE). Use TestToolLoopAgent and TwoToolThenText-style fakes from ParallelToolExecutionTest. Assert CancellationException escapes and no ToolResult/ToolError event was emitted for the cancelled call.
confirmed.dimension: Testability & composability gaps

verdict.critique: Evidence confirmed by reading ToolLoopAgent.kt:1395-1404 — the executeTool body has an explicit `catch (ce: CancellationException) { throw ce }` ordered before `catch (t: Throwable)` which calls `toolFailure(call, t)` producing a durable ToolExecutionResult.Failure. The invariant (a cancelled tool body must abort, not be recorded as a Failure and continue) is real and correctness-critical. I tried to refute the no-test claim by examining all 7 test files that reference CancellationException: UIMessageShapeTest:139-147 covers `createUiMessageStream` (a separate UI stream builder, NOT executeTool); ToolLoopAgentSupersessionTest cancels an engine job with an EMPTY toolSet (no tool body throws CE); AgentSessionTest is the same supersession scenario; CompletionApiTest/TelemetryWiringTest/ChatConcurrencyTest/WithRealTimeoutTest cover transport/hook/chat/timeout cancellation. None makes a tool's execute() throw CancellationException inside a running agent step and asserts it escapes generate()/stream() with no Failure persisted. So the gap is genuine. The solution (add a commonTest with TestToolLoopAgent) is idiomatic and the supporting fakes (TestToolLoopAgent.kt, ParallelToolExecutionTest patterns) exist. abiAssessment=false is trivially correct (test-only). tenetAligned: T7 (hard-to-test risky path) — adding a test is the right move; no sealed/Flow contract concerns. One refinement: the original asserts only on stream events; the actual invariant is about NOT persisting a Failure into the conversation, so the test should ALSO assert engineState.messages contains no tool-result/Failure ContentPart for the cancelled call, not just the absence of a StreamEvent. Severity 'medium' is defensible-but-generous: the path is currently well-guarded (explicit ordered catch + load-bearing comment), so the actual present-day risk is a future-refactor regression, which is exactly what a test pins.
verdict.refinedSolution:
Add `src/commonTest/kotlin/ai/torad/aisdk/ToolCancellationTest.kt` with two cases using TestToolLoopAgent and a one/two-tool fake model (TwoToolThenText pattern from ParallelToolExecutionTest):

Case 1 (single tool throws CE):
- Build a tool whose execute body does `throw CancellationException("x")`.
- Collect the stream from agent.stream(...) inside `assertFailsWith<CancellationException> { ... }`.
- Assert the CE escapes, AND assert BOTH layers reflect non-persistence:
  (a) no StreamEvent.ToolResult / ToolError was emitted for that toolCallId, and
  (b) agent.engineState.value.messages contains no ToolExecutionResult.Failure / tool-result ContentPart for that toolCallId (this is the real invariant — cancellation must not become a durable tool result that the loop continues on).
- Assert engineState.error is null/non-Failure (cancellation is not an error result).

Case 2 (parallel path, one of two tools throws CE):
- Two-tool step, tool A throws CE, tool B is a slow no-op.
- Assert `assertFailsWith<CancellationException>`, that the surrounding coroutineScope tears down (tool B's output is NOT persisted either), and no Failure ToolResult is appended for either call.

Run on the StandardTestDispatcher(testScheduler) engine context (as the supersession/parallel tests do) for deterministic ordering, using runTest + advanceUntilIdle. No production code changes; ABI untouched (breaksAbi=false). This locks the catch-ordering invariant against a future refactor that widens `catch (t: Throwable)` to swallow CE.
