confirmed.dimension: Testability & composability gaps

verdict.critique:
All four cited evidence snippets are accurate when opened. (a) Refutations of claims #9/#10 are CORRECT: executeTool (Tool.kt:845) is a streaming flow with one-step lookahead via pendingFinal — it emits Preliminary for the prior value then Final, never buffering the full stream. ToolLoopAgent.kt:937-1019 is a genuine streaming engine (coroutineScope + Channel(BUFFERED) + Semaphore.withPermit + ordered incremental applyToolResult keyed by nextToApply), not awaitAll/BufferingCollector. (b) Claim #11 is genuinely fixed: Chat.kt:175 and Streams.kt:112 both catch CancellationException and rethrow before the broad Throwable catch. The ABI assessment (breaksAbi=false) is right — this is a no-op/test-only finding that touches no public signatures. Tenet-wise nothing is violated.

The solution is marked incorrect on one load-bearing point: gap (c) recommends adding a negative test for the tampered-replay signature path AS IF MISSING, but it already exists. ToolApprovalSignatureWiringTest.kt:111 (`tampering with the replayed input fails closed`) asserts AgentError.InvalidToolApprovalSignature is thrown and `state.executed.isEmpty()`; ToolApprovalSignatureWiringTest.kt:137 adds a second InvalidToolApprovalSignature assertion; ToolApprovalSignatureTest.kt:43-50 unit-tests tampered input → verifyToolApprovalSignature returns false. The finding's own distinction ("wiring test but no negative unit test") is too thin to justify new work — the fail-closed behavior of verifyReplayedApprovalSignature/revalidateApprovedInput is already covered end-to-end. The OTHER half of (c) is valid: ParallelToolExecutionTest.kt has only success-path tests (lines 45/77/96/152); there is genuinely no test for a tool throwing a non-CE Throwable concurrently with a slow sibling, exercising the ordered Failure-apply path in the coroutineScope at ToolLoopAgent.kt:1004-1019.

This is a meta verification finding; its primary deliverable (do NOT action #9/#10/#11; mark the HTML report stale) is verified correct and valuable. It is downgraded only because one of its two concrete test suggestions is already done.
verdict.refinedSolution:
Keep the no-action verdict on (a)/(b): claims #9 (Promise.all/awaitAll buffering) and #10 (whole-flow buffering) are refuted by Tool.kt:845 and ToolLoopAgent.kt:937-1019; claim #11 (cancellation→UI error) is fixed at Chat.kt:175 / Streams.kt:112. Mark those report claims stale; do not modify executeTool or the parallel engine.

For (c), DROP the signature/revalidateApprovedInput negative-test recommendation — it is already covered by ToolApprovalSignatureWiringTest.kt:111 and :137 (both assert AgentError.InvalidToolApprovalSignature on tampered replay + non-execution) and ToolApprovalSignatureTest.kt:43-50 (tampered input → verify returns false). Citing it as a gap is incorrect.

Add ONLY the genuinely-missing test: a parallel-failure ordering test in ParallelToolExecutionTest.kt where one tool throws a non-CancellationException Throwable while a slower sibling is still running. Assert that (1) the throwing tool surfaces as ToolExecutionResult.Failure applied in original call order via the nextToApply loop (ToolLoopAgent.kt:1010-1019), (2) the slow sibling still completes and its result is applied, and (3) the surrounding coroutineScope does not propagate the tool failure as an uncaught exception that tears down the sibling (i.e. executeTool's internal catch converts the throw to Failure rather than cancelling the scope). This closes the only real coverage gap and is test-only (breaksAbi=false). Low priority given the existing end-to-end coverage.

gaps.area: AiSdkError.kt error hierarchy never opened — the largest T1 violation in the codebase is uncovered
gaps.why: AiSdkException is a `public open class` (AiSdkError.kt:3) with ~40 flat subtypes declared `public class X : AiSdkException` and NO `sealed` keyword anywhere in the file (verified: grep for `sealed` returns nothing). This makes an exhaustive `when (e)` over SDK errors impossible — a direct T1 violation (sealed source of truth, exhaustive when) that the entire SEAL dimension missed because AiSdkError.kt was never opened. AgentError IS sealed (AgentError.kt:43), proving the pattern was known and just not applied to the core tree. Compounding: 10 `Any?` payload fields inside error types (requestBodyValues/data at lines 19/25, prompt:31, data:37, value:73/79/97, chunk:185, content:233, originalMessage:245) — the same SEAL-02 Any?-erasure pattern but in errors, uncounted; plus `typealias AISDKError = AiSdkException` (line 8) is a TS-name-residue typealias the PO dimension didn't list. ABI note: converting `open class`→`sealed class` is binary-breaking (subclassing was permitted) and MUST be batched into the Phase-2 rebaseline; the catalog never flags this.
gaps.suggestedDimension: Sealed modeling: add SEAL-07 — seal AiSdkException into a sealed class hierarchy (or a sealed wrapper over the leaf exceptions), generify/replace the 10 Any? error payloads, and deprecate the AISDKError typealias. State binary-compat impact explicitly.

gaps.area: LangChain.kt / LlamaIndex.kt are TS-ecosystem adapter packages the FW dimension skipped
gaps.why: FW-1..6 scoped framework residue to react/rsc/svelte/vue/angular only, but LangChain.kt (434 lines) and LlamaIndex.kt are ports of @ai-sdk/langchain and @ai-sdk/llamaindex stream adapters — equally JS-ecosystem-shaped. They carry two anti-patterns the catalog never names: (1) callback-bags — `StreamCallbacks` (LlamaIndex.kt:15) is 7 nullable suspend lambdas (onStart/onToken/onText/onFinish/onError/onAbort…), the canonical T4 violation (callbacks instead of returning a composable Flow), and the adapters already return `Flow<UIMessage>` so the callback bag is pure TS-mimicry; (2) sentinel id fabrication — `assistantMessageId: String = "1"` (LlamaIndex.kt:28,34) and `= "langchain-msg-1"` (LangChain.kt:142,148) are exactly the T5 fabrication + primitive-obsession (String id) the audit flags elsewhere, here unflagged.
gaps.suggestedDimension: TS framework packages in a KMP library: extend with FW-7 (LangChain/LlamaIndex adapters) — drop StreamCallbacks in favor of the Flow the adapter already returns (T4), and replace the fabricated default ids with a required/typed message-id parameter.

gaps.area: The entire non-text model surface (embeddings, reranking, media) was never audited
gaps.why: The catalog is 100% text-generation + UI. Embedding.kt, Rerank.kt, MediaModels.kt (679 lines) and ~15 media providers (ElevenLabs, Deepgram, Fal, Luma, Kling, BlackForestLabs, Prodia, Replicate, Hume, Gladia, RevAI, AssemblyAI, LMNT, Voyage) are entirely uncovered yet exhibit the same anti-patterns the catalog fixes for text: MediaModels.kt has 76 `= null,` nullable-default fields (NOB option-bag pattern); Embedding.kt and Rerank.kt expose `providerOptions`/`providerMetadata: Map<String, JsonElement>` (Rerank.kt:17/33/43, Embedding.kt:38/49/64/76) — the exact raw-json leakage raw-json-01..04 only addresses on the text path. Any fix to providerOptions/metadata typing that ignores this surface will leave the boundary half-migrated (a dependency-scan red flag).
gaps.suggestedDimension: Nullable option-bags + Raw JSON leakage: widen NOB and raw-json scope to embedding/rerank/media so the typed-provider-data boundary lands across ALL model surfaces in one cut, not just text.

gaps.area: The named 'Coroutines/Flow correctness' dimension was only exercised on experimental web-framework residue
gaps.why: The only Flow findings are FW-4 (StreamableValueController.error) and FW-5 (snapshot getters) — both inside the rsc/react code slated for deletion. The actual core streaming/backpressure path was never opened: HttpTransport.kt:265 (`channelFlow` SSE producer), KtorGatewayTransport.kt (810 lines), SmoothStream.kt:115 (`upstream.buffer().collect`), ui/Streams.kt:90 (`channelFlow`), AmazonBedrockProvider.kt:1216 (`channelFlow`), ui/MessageStreamReader.kt, ui/ToUIMessageStream.kt. These are where real cancellation/buffering/backpressure bugs live. The code shows deliberate care (comments at HttpTransport.kt:266-314, MCP.kt:471 noting structured-scope-over-GlobalScope), so the dimension may largely pass — but the audit cannot claim coverage of cancellation/buffering/suspend-vs-Flow without having read the producers. Verified non-issue worth recording: GlobalScope is only referenced in comments (never used), and companion objects total just 6 — so 'companion overuse' is a non-finding, not a gap.
gaps.suggestedDimension: Coroutines/Flow correctness: open the core streaming producers (HttpTransport, KtorGatewayTransport, SmoothStream, Streams, ToUIMessageStream, MessageStreamReader) and confirm channelFlow send-safety, cancellation propagation through buffer(), and that backpressure is bounded — then either issue findings or explicitly certify the dimension clean with file:line evidence.

gaps.area: God-files / structural concentration (T8) is named but has zero dedicated findings
gaps.why: The catalog references ToolLoopAgent.kt line ranges but never flags file size. Measured: MCP.kt 2221, GoogleProvider.kt 2044, ToolLoopAgent.kt 1881, AmazonBedrockProvider.kt 1583, OpenAICompatibleProviderFacades.kt 1505, OpenAICompatibleProvider.kt 1325, Tool.kt 1247. MCP.kt is the single largest file, a stateful network protocol with `CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob()+Dispatchers.Default)` (MCP.kt:473) and four engineContext-holding classes, and it was never opened — both a god-file and a mutable-state surface the GLOBAL dimension missed. T7 (a hard-to-test private method signals an unextracted unit) and T8 deserve at least one finding pinning the concrete split boundaries.
gaps.suggestedDimension: God files & structural concentration: add GOD-01 for MCP.kt and the >1500-line providers, with feature-slice split proposals (T8) and a note on which giant private blocks block testability (T7). All splits are ABI-safe if public symbols are preserved.

gaps.area: raw-json findings under-scope a 225-occurrence pervasive boundary
gaps.why: raw-json-01..04 read as targeted (provider tool info, hosted-tool config, text providerOptions/metadata). But `Map<String, JsonElement>` appears 225 times across the source — this is THE de-facto provider/wire boundary (T2) running through text, embedding, rerank, media, tools, and result metadata. Treating it as four scoped findings risks a half-migration where some surfaces get a typed `ProviderMetadata` value class and others keep the raw map (a classic two-patterns-coexist smell). The audit should quantify the full footprint and prescribe one boundary type applied everywhere.
gaps.suggestedDimension: Provider/wire boundary & typed protocol states: reframe raw-json as a single cross-surface boundary migration (one ProviderOptions/ProviderMetadata typed seam + per-provider serializers) with the 225-site footprint stated, so wire-vs-domain (T2) settles once.

critic.overallAssessment: The catalog is strong and adversarially verified within its scope, but its scope is narrower than the 13 declared dimensions admit. Three whole areas were never opened: (1) the error model — AiSdkException is a flat `open class` tree of ~40 subtypes with no `sealed` keyword and 10 `Any?` payloads, the single largest unaddressed T1 violation, missed because AiSdkError.kt was never read; (2) the entire non-text surface — Embedding/Rerank/MediaModels (76 nullable fields) and ~15 media providers carry the identical option-bag and Map<String,JsonElement> patterns the catalog fixes only for text; (3) the LangChain/LlamaIndex ecosystem adapters, which are framework residue (callback-bags + fabricated string ids) outside the five web packages FW covered. Two named dimensions are asserted but thinly evidenced: 'Coroutines/Flow correctness' was exercised only on the to-be-deleted rsc/react code while the real streaming producers (HttpTransport/KtorGatewayTransport/SmoothStream channelFlow paths) went unread; and 'God files' (T8) has no dedicated finding despite MCP.kt at 2221 lines and six files over 1200. On the positive side I can certify two non-issues with evidence: companion-object overuse does not exist (6 total) and GlobalScope is referenced only in explanatory comments, never used — so the catalog correctly did not flag them. The raw-json footprint (225 Map<String,JsonElement> sites) is materially larger than the four scoped findings imply and should be reframed as one boundary migration to avoid a half-done state.
critic.topLeveragePoint: Establish ONE typed provider-data boundary — replace `Map<String, JsonElement>` (225 occurrences) for providerOptions/providerMetadata with a typed `ProviderOptions`/`ProviderMetadata` value type plus per-provider serializers — and apply it across EVERY model surface at once (text, embedding, rerank, media, tools, result metadata), not just the text path the raw-json findings target. This is the highest-leverage move because it is the codebase's pervasive T2 wire-vs-domain seam: it simultaneously eliminates the bulk of the Map erasure and a large share of the `Any?` fields, settles the wire-vs-domain boundary in one place so downstream sealed-modeling and typed-result work can rely on it, and — critically — prevents the half-migration the current four-finding scoping would otherwise produce. Sequence it as a single deliberate ABI rebaseline (it is binary-breaking) after the sealed-modeling batch lands so the types settle exactly once.
